---

# Locality Guide — Pre-Statehood Alabama (c. 1780–1820)

## Jurisdictional Context

Your ancestor lived in territory that passed through several governing hands before Alabama statehood in 1819:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1702–1763 | French colonial (Mobile area) | French settlement concentrated around Mobile Bay |
| 1763–1780 | British West Florida | Britain acquired the territory after the Seven Years' War |
| 1780–1813 | Spanish West Florida / Mobile District | Spain controlled Mobile until U.S. seizure in 1813 |
| 1798–1817 | **Mississippi Territory** | American territorial government; the primary jurisdiction for records c. 1800 |
| 1817–1819 | Alabama Territory | Split from Mississippi Territory before statehood |
| 1819– | State of Alabama | |

**County formation is critical to locate records.** At 1800, virtually all of present-day Alabama fell within **Washington County, Mississippi Territory** (formed 1800). The territory subdivided rapidly: **Madison County** was carved out in 1808, **Baldwin** in 1809, and **Mobile County** in 1812. You must trace the correct county for your ancestor's location to find where court, probate, and deed records were created. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Mississippi Territory Genealogy — County Formation")*

---

## Primary Repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH)

📍 Montgomery, Alabama | [archives.alabama.gov](https://archives.alabama.gov)

The ADAH is the single most important repository for this period. It holds:
- **Mississippi Territory census substitutes**: territorial enumerations taken in **1801, 1805, 1808, 1810, and 1816** are held here (also at MDAH — see below). These are the closest thing to a "census" for your ancestor around 1800 and are **not digitized on FamilySearch** — physical access or correspondence required.
- Territorial tax lists and militia rolls
- Early county court records from Washington, Madison, Baldwin, and Mobile counties (some beginning c. 1800)
- Microfilm copies of Spanish colonial records for the Mobile District

---

## Primary Repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History (MDAH)

📍 Jackson, Mississippi | [mdah.ms.gov](https://www.mdah.ms.gov)

Because the Mississippi Territory government was seated at Natchez, Mississippi, many territorial records — including **the 1801, 1805, 1808, 1810, and 1816 census enumerations** — are also held at MDAH. The MDAH maintains the territorial government's administrative papers alongside the ADAH holdings. Researchers should check both repositories.

---

## National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)

📍 Multiple locations | [archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov)

- **Land Entry Files (Record Group 49)**: Federal land sales in the Alabama portion of the Mississippi Territory began in **1803** through the **St. Stephens Land Office** (southwest Alabama) and the **Huntsville Land Office** (north Alabama). Original entry case files are at NARA; many are also indexed on the **BLM General Land Office (GLO) website** at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov) — this is a free, searchable online index and a critical first stop for land research.
- **Military Records**: Service records and pension files for the **War of 1812** and the **Creek War (1813–1814)** — both directly involving present-day Alabama settlers — are at NARA. Fold3 ([fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com)) has digitized portions of these.
- **Territorial Papers of the United States**: Published documentary series covering the Mississippi Territory (Vols. V–VI); many are available in university libraries.

---

## FamilySearch Digitized Collections (c. 1780–1820 window)

FamilySearch holds **4 indexed Alabama collections**, but most begin at **1809 or later**, meaning direct coverage of the c. 1800 period is thin. Here is what the tool returned:

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1598408) | 1809–1950 | 612,345 | 306,172 | **Indexed + images.** Starts 1809 — captures the very tail end of the territorial period. |
| [Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1537764) | 1809–1985 | 438,210 | 219,105 | **Indexed + images.** Estates filed from 1809 onward; an ancestor alive ~1800 may appear in early probate after statehood. |
| [Alabama, Compiled Census & Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2016756) | 1810–1890 | 287,634 | None | **Index only, no images.** Begins 1810; includes compiled substitutes that may incorporate pre-statehood tax lists and enumerations. |
| [Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1986396) | 1803–1950 | 156,789 | 78,394 | **Indexed + images.** Earliest coverage starts 1803 — the most likely FamilySearch source for an ancestor present ~1800. |

⚠️ **Key gap:** No FamilySearch collection covers the full territorial period before 1803. For 1798–1803, you are working almost entirely with physical/microfilm records at ADAH and MDAH.

---

## Record Types by Category

### Land Records ⭐ *Highest priority for this period*
- **BLM GLO**: Free, searchable index of federal land patents from the St. Stephens and Huntsville land offices (from 1803). Start here.
- **NARA RG 49**: Original entry files with more personal detail than the patent itself.
- **Spanish colonial land grants** (Mobile area, pre-1813): Copies at the Library of Congress and ADAH. Originals at the Archivo General de Indias, Seville.
- **British land grants** (1763–1780): Some in NARA; some in the British National Archives (Kew).

### Census & Population Substitutes
- **Mississippi Territory enumerations** (1801, 1805, 1808, 1810, 1816): ADAH and MDAH — physical/microfilm only.
- **1810 federal census** for Mississippi Territory includes present-day Alabama — this is searchable via FamilySearch's Census Substitutes collection (above) and Ancestry.
- Tax lists serve as census substitutes for intercensal years; held at ADAH.

### Vital Records
- **No civil registration** existed in the territorial period. Births, marriages, and deaths were recorded only by churches.
- The FamilySearch [Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1986396) collection is your primary option. Methodist and Baptist circuit-rider records are the most common denominations for this frontier period.
- Marriage bonds and licenses in county clerk offices begin with county formation: Washington Co. from ~1800, Madison Co. from 1808.

### Church Records
- Baptist and Methodist circuit records, some digitized in the FamilySearch church records collection above.
- **Catholic parish registers** (Mobile area): St. Louis Cathedral registers beginning in the colonial period, held at the Archdiocese of Mobile and in microfilm at ADAH and the Mobile Municipal Archives.
- **French colonial records**: Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, Aix-en-Provence (France); microfilm copies at the Mobile Municipal Archives. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Alabama Genealogy — Colonial Records")*
- **Spanish sacramental registers**: Archivo General de Indias, Seville; copies at the Library of Congress. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Alabama Genealogy — Colonial Records")*

### Military Records
- **Territorial militia rolls**: ADAH and MDAH.
- **War of 1812 / Creek War service records and pension files**: NARA; Fold3 has digitized portions.
- Many settlers in pre-statehood Alabama came through military service — a pension file may be the richest biographical document surviving for this period.

### Probate & Court Records
- Washington County court records from ~1800: ADAH holds these; also on FamilySearch's Probate collection beginning 1809.
- Territorial superior court records: ADAH.

### Newspapers
- Newspapers in Alabama before statehood were scarce. The *Mobile Gazette* began around 1813. [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com) and [Chronicling America](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) (free) have some early Alabama titles, but coverage is thin before 1820.

---

## External Resources Summary

From the FamilySearch curated links (6 resources returned):

| Resource | Best Use |
|---|---|
| [Ancestry.com — Federal Census](https://www.ancestry.com/search/collections/census/) | 1810 census (first to cover present-day Alabama) |
| [NARA](https://www.archives.gov) | Land entry files, military service/pension records |
| [Fold3](https://www.fold3.com) | War of 1812, Creek War military records |
| [Find A Grave](https://www.findagrave.com) | Cemetery records (useful for post-statehood death dates of c. 1800 settlers) |
| [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com) | Limited pre-1820 coverage; useful for later confirmation |
| [FamilySearch Wiki — Vital Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Category:Vital_Records) | Starting-point guidance |

---

## Summary: Where to Begin

Given the pre-statehood window, here is the recommended priority order:

1. **BLM GLO website** — free, online, indexed land patents from 1803; highest chance of finding your ancestor by name.
2. **ADAH / MDAH** — territorial census enumerations (1801, 1805, 1808) and tax lists; not online, require direct access or correspondence.
3. **FamilySearch — Alabama, Compiled Census & Census Substitutes Index (1810–1890)** — compiled substitutes may reach back into the territorial period.
4. **FamilySearch — Alabama, Church Records (1803–1950)** — best option for vital events; check the Mobile area Catholic registers separately.
5. **NARA** — military pension files (extremely rich biographical sources) and land entry case files.
6. **County courthouse records** — trace which county your ancestor's location fell in at the relevant date, then check ADAH for those county-level deed and court records.

Would you like a research plan targeting a specific ancestor, county, or record type for this period?

---

# Locality Guide: Arkansas Region (Pre-1800, Spanish Colonial Period)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Governing authority:** The Arkansas region was never a distinct colonial jurisdiction — it was administered as a district within the **Province of Louisiana** (*Provincia de Luisiana*), governed from New Orleans. Records flow upward to that capital, not to any local Arkansas authority.
- **Administrative center:** Arkansas Post (*Post de Arkansas* / *Poste des Arkansas*), established 1686 by the French on the lower Arkansas River — the only significant European settlement in the region throughout this period.
- **Jurisdictional sequence:**
  - 1686–1762: **French Louisiana** (La Louisiane) — Catholic mission/fur trade colony
  - 1762/63–1800: **Spanish Louisiana** — transferred by secret Treaty of Fontainebleau (1762), formalized by Treaty of Paris (1763)
  - 1800–1803: Briefly retroceded to **Napoleonic France** (Treaty of San Ildefonso)
  - 1803: **Louisiana Purchase** — U.S. control begins
  - 1819: Arkansas Territory organized; 1836: Arkansas statehood
- **Population:** No population data is available from tools for this period. European settlers numbered in the low hundreds; the population was predominantly Native American (Quapaw, Osage, Caddo nations), with a small community of French creole traders, their mixed-heritage families, Spanish soldiers and officials, and enslaved Africans.
- **Economy:** Fur trade, subsistence agriculture, military garrison.
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic exclusively — French Capuchin and Jesuit missionaries, later Spanish Franciscans and diocesan priests.
- **Languages of record:** **French** (French period and persisting into Spanish era among settlers) and **Spanish** (official colonial administration after 1763). Researchers must be able to read both.

---

## Boundary changes

- The Arkansas region had no internal county or parish boundaries during this period — it was a single administrative district (*distrito*) within the larger Province of Louisiana.
- The governing seat was **New Orleans**; the Lieutenant-Governor for the upper Louisiana district was based at **St. Louis** (after Spain reorganized the province c. 1770). Records for Arkansas Post moved through this chain of command.
- When researching this area, treat the **Province of Louisiana** (Spain) as the governing jurisdiction — its records are the primary source, not any Arkansas-level archive.
- After 1803, American authorities confirmed valid Spanish and French land grants; those proceedings created a second layer of U.S.-era documentation that can help reconstruct pre-1800 land tenure.

---

## Available record types

### Church records ⬅ **Primary genealogical source for this period**

- **Denominations:** Roman Catholic only. French Capuchin and Jesuit missionaries operated at Arkansas Post from the early 1700s; the post passed to Spanish ecclesiastical authority after 1763.
- **Record types:** Baptisms, marriages, and burials — covering European settlers, mixed-heritage individuals, and some Native American and enslaved persons.
- **FamilySearch digitized volume:** One image group (imageGroupNumber **008814402**) covers **Arkansas Post Sacramental Registers (Spanish Colonial), 1763–1800** — **64 images**, in **Spanish**, **0% name-indexed**, **not full-text searchable**. Access level: **browse-only images**. Researchers must scroll through every image; fluency in Spanish colonial ecclesiastical handwriting is required.
- **Physical repositories:**
  - **Roman Catholic Diocese of Little Rock** — holds surviving original registers
  - **Arkansas History Commission** (Little Rock) — holds transcribed/abstracted versions
- **Note:** French-period registers (pre-1763) are fragmentary and are not represented in the FamilySearch volume above. The Arkansas Genealogy wiki article (source: *Arkansas Genealogy #Church Records*) confirms surviving French-era registers are also held by the Diocese of Little Rock and Arkansas History Commission.
- **Language warning:** Records are in Latin (sacramental formulas) with names and notes in French or Spanish. Do not expect English-language entries.

---

### Colonial census / population enumerations (*padrones*)

- Spanish colonial administrators conducted periodic *padrones* (household censuses) of Arkansas Post, listing heads of household, family members, enslaved persons, and sometimes ages and origins.
- **No FamilySearch indexed collection** exists for these enumerations. They are **not** included in the *Arkansas Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1819–1870* (FamilySearch collection **2085124**), which begins after the U.S. territorial period.
- **Where held:**
  - **Archivo General de Indias (AGI)**, Seville, Spain — the principal repository for all Spanish colonial administrative records, including *padrones* for Louisiana. The relevant section is *Papeles de Cuba* (Papers of Cuba), which contains the administrative files of the colonial governors of Louisiana and the Floridas. **Physical visit or document request required; some finding aids are available online at** [pares.mcu.es](https://pares.mcu.es).
  - **Library of Congress** (Washington, D.C.) — holds microfilm copies of selected AGI records relating to Louisiana and Florida, including some Arkansas Post materials. Accessible through the Library's reading room or interlibrary loan for microfilm.
  - **Arkansas History Commission** — holds some transcriptions and abstracts of *padrón* data.
- **Access level: Physical only / microfilm** for most materials; **not available online** through any genealogical database.

---

### Land records

- Spain issued land concessions (*concesiones de tierras*) to settlers at Arkansas Post and along the Arkansas River. These grants were administered through the colonial governor's office in New Orleans.
- After 1803, the U.S. Board of Land Commissioners (created 1805) adjudicated private land claims derived from French and Spanish grants. **These confirmation records are the most accessible entry point** for reconstructing pre-1800 land tenure.
- **Where held:**
  - **U.S. Bureau of Land Management General Land Office (BLM GLO)** — [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov) — contains confirmed American-era patents; search for "private land claims" in Arkansas. **Free; searchable online.**
  - **NARA** — Record Group 49 (Bureau of Land Management) and Record Group 59 (State Department) include the territorial land claims files.
  - **Archivo General de Indias (AGI)**, *Papeles de Cuba* — original Spanish concession documents. Physical/microfilm only.
  - **Arkansas History Commission** — holds abstracts and some original documents relating to Spanish land grants.
- **Access level:** BLM GLO post-1803 confirmations — **indexed + images online (free)**; original Spanish grants — **physical or microfilm only**.

---

### Military records

- Arkansas Post was a garrisoned military post throughout the Spanish period. The garrison included Spanish regular troops and locally recruited militia.
- Record types: muster rolls (*listas de revista*), service records, correspondence between the post commander and the governor in New Orleans.
- **Where held:** **Archivo General de Indias**, *Papeles de Cuba* section. Also **Archivo General de Simancas** (Spain) for some military service records. **Physical or ordered microfilm only.**
- **U.S. connection:** Some Spanish colonial officers later served under American authority after 1803; their pension or service records may appear at NARA.
- The FamilySearch collection **Fold3 — Military Records** (curated external link returned for this place) covers U.S. military records and does not extend to the Spanish colonial period.

---

### Probate and court records

- Formal probate as understood in U.S. law did not exist. Estates were handled through the Spanish colonial *alcalde* (local judge) and records forwarded to the governor in New Orleans.
- The **Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984** collection (FamilySearch collection **1798054**, 456,789 records, 228,394 images) covers Louisiana broadly and begins in the colonial period — it may include a small number of estate or court proceedings touching Arkansas Post settlers administered through New Orleans. Worth checking, but coverage for the Arkansas district specifically is uncertain.
- **Where held:** Primarily **AGI** *Papeles de Cuba* and **Louisiana State Archives** / **New Orleans Notarial Archives** for any notarized documents.

---

### Vital records (civil registration)

- **Did not exist** for this period. Spain and France did not maintain civil (government) vital registration in their North American colonies. The Catholic church register is the sole source for births/baptisms, marriages, and burials — see Church Records above.
- U.S. civil registration in Arkansas began only in 1914 (statewide). County-level registration began in the 1830s but is entirely post-period.

---

### Census records (U.S.)

- The first U.S. federal census covering Arkansas was **1830** (Arkansas Territory was missed in 1820; a partial 1820 census exists). All are post-period.
- The **Arkansas Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1819–1870** (FamilySearch collection **2085124**, 178,345 records) begins in the territorial period — post-period for Spanish colonial research, but useful for tracking individuals who were alive before 1800 and appear in the earliest American-era enumerations.
- No U.S. census data applies to the pre-1800 period.

---

### Newspaper records

- No newspapers were published in the Arkansas region before 1800. The nearest colonial press was in New Orleans, which began publishing in the 1790s.
- **Not applicable** for the pre-1800 Arkansas district.

---

### Immigration / emigration records

- No formal immigration records were kept for settlers arriving in Spanish Louisiana. Travelers and settlers entered through New Orleans; some passenger lists for New Orleans arrivals survive in the AGI and in the **New Orleans Notarial Archives**.
- **Canary Island immigration (1778–1783):** Spain actively recruited settlers from the Canary Islands (*Isleños*) to populate Louisiana; some were directed to Arkansas Post. Their contracts and passenger lists may be in the **AGI** and in transcribed published genealogies of the *Isleños*.
- **Not available online** as a named genealogical database collection.

---

### DNA and genetic genealogy

- For researchers who cannot document lineage through the sparse paper record, autosomal and Y-chromosome DNA can help identify colonial-era French creole and Spanish connections through matches to descendants of known colonial families in Louisiana, Missouri (also part of Spanish Louisiana), and the Gulf Coast.
- The **Louisiana French Creole** and **Acadian** communities share genetic heritage with Arkansas Post settlers; testing through AncestryDNA or 23andMe and filtering for Louisiana/Gulf Coast matches may surface family connections.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Relevant holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Arkansas Post Sacramental Registers 1763–1800 (64 images, browse-only, Spanish); Louisiana Church Records 1718–1925 (may include some Arkansas content); Louisiana Probate Records 1756–1984 | Free |
| **BLM General Land Office** ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) | Post-1803 confirmed land patents and private land claims derived from Spanish grants | Free |
| **NARA Online (Fold3, Ancestry)** | U.S. territorial and early statehood records (post-1803); some colonial records via NARA digitization projects | Subscription (Fold3/Ancestry) or free at NARA |
| **Archivo General de Indias — PARES** ([pares.mcu.es](https://pares.mcu.es)) | Finding aids and some digitized documents from *Papeles de Cuba* (Louisiana colonial records) | Free to browse finding aids; some images online |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Arkansas History Commission** (Little Rock, AR) | Transcripts of Spanish colonial records; land records; church register abstracts; territorial-era documents | In-person; some materials by mail/email request; [arkansasheritage.com](https://www.arkansasheritage.com) |
| **Roman Catholic Diocese of Little Rock** | Original and microfilmed sacramental registers for Arkansas Post | Contact the Diocesan Archives; appointment required |
| **Archivo General de Indias** (Seville, Spain) | *Papeles de Cuba* — the complete administrative archive of Spanish Louisiana including padrones, land concessions, military records, correspondence for Arkansas Post | In-person or commissioned researcher; some records on microfilm at Library of Congress |
| **Library of Congress** (Washington, D.C.) | Microfilm of selected AGI Louisiana records; manuscript collections relating to the colonial Mississippi Valley | Reading room; some microfilm available via interlibrary loan |
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives** (New Orleans, LA) | Notarized colonial documents; some Arkansas Post settlers conducted legal business in New Orleans | In-person; partial online finding aids |
| **Louisiana State Archives** (Baton Rouge, LA) | Colonial-era Louisiana records, some touching Arkansas district; early American-era records for the region | In-person; some digital finding aids |
| **Missouri History Museum / State Archives** | Records of Upper Louisiana district (based at St. Louis), which administered Arkansas Post during portions of the Spanish period | In-person |

---

## Records NOT online

The following record types are confirmed to exist but are **not available** in any online genealogical database:

- **Spanish colonial padrones (censuses)** of Arkansas Post — held at AGI Seville; Library of Congress microfilm
- **Original Spanish land concession documents** — held at AGI Seville
- **Military muster rolls and garrison records** for Arkansas Post — held at AGI Seville
- **French-period sacramental registers (pre-1763)** — held at Diocese of Little Rock; Arkansas History Commission
- **Colonial court/estate proceedings** administered through New Orleans — held at New Orleans Notarial Archives and Louisiana State Archives
- **Canary Islander emigration contracts** — held at AGI Seville

---

## Known record losses

- Survival of Arkansas Post records is **fragmentary**. The post changed hands multiple times and experienced periods of abandonment and flooding. Many records that should exist were never transferred to a permanent archive or were lost to fire, flood, or neglect.
- The **64-image FamilySearch volume** (imageGroupNumber 008814402) represents a small surviving slice of what was created during 37 years of Spanish colonial administration — treat every name found in it as significant.
- The AGI *Papeles de Cuba* series is large (tens of thousands of legajos) but not fully digitized; researchers should expect to commission a professional researcher in Seville or work through the Library of Congress microfilm program.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the one online foothold:** Browse FamilySearch imageGroupNumber **008814402** (Arkansas Post Sacramental Registers, 1763–1800). At 64 images and 0% indexed, it is small enough to read cover to cover. All entries are in Spanish; expect columnar sacramental register format with Latin formulas and Spanish/French names.

2. **Work the Louisiana collections for the governing jurisdiction.** Because Arkansas was administered from New Orleans, check the **Louisiana Church Records, 1718–1925** (FamilySearch collection 1612905) and **Louisiana Probate Records, 1756–1984** (collection 1798054) — Arkansas Post settlers sometimes appear in records created at the colonial capital.

3. **Use post-1803 land claims to reconstruct pre-1800 residents.** The U.S. Board of Land Commissioners' proceedings (NARA RG 49) required claimants to document their Spanish-era grants. These testimonies often name the original grantee, date of settlement, and family members — excellent proxy evidence for the pre-1800 period.

4. **Consult the *Papeles de Cuba* at the AGI or via Library of Congress microfilm.** This is the master archive for Spanish Louisiana. For Arkansas Post specifically, look for legajos filed under "Arkansas" or "Arco" (a common abbreviation). The LOC's Hispanic Division can assist with navigating the microfilm holdings.

5. **Published transcriptions exist.** Scholars have transcribed portions of Arkansas colonial records. Key published works to locate through interlibrary loan:
   - Morris S. Arnold's works on colonial Arkansas (*Colonial Arkansas, 1686–1804*) include demographic analysis and name lists drawn from archival sources.
   - The *Arkansas Historical Quarterly* has published translations of Spanish padrones and land grant abstracts.

6. **Language preparation:** Records alternate between French (settlers' language) and Spanish (official administrative language). Latin appears in church registers. A researcher without reading knowledge of at least one of these languages will need professional translation or transcription assistance.

7. **Consider neighboring jurisdictions.** Settlers from Arkansas Post also appear in records from **Natchez** (Mississippi), **New Orleans** (Louisiana), and **Ste. Genevieve / St. Louis** (Missouri) — all part of Spanish Louisiana. A person who left no record at Arkansas Post may appear in the records of a neighboring post they visited for trade, legal proceedings, or marriage.

8. **Source:** Much of the above guidance is drawn from the FamilySearch Wiki article *Arkansas Genealogy*, sections "Colonial Records" and "Church Records" ([https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Arkansas_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Arkansas_Genealogy)).

Good — `standardPlace` is **"Ohio, United States"** (state formed 1803). Since the target is 1790 (pre-statehood Northwest Territory), I'll now fire the full parallel survey batch.Now I have everything I need to compile the guide. Here is the full survey, grounded entirely in tool results.

---

# Locality Guide: Ohio (c. 1790, Pre-Statehood / Northwest Territory Era)

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status in 1790** | Unincorporated territory under the **Northwest Territory**, created by the Northwest Ordinance of 1787 |
| **First county organized** | Washington County, 1788 (covered a vast swath of present-day eastern Ohio) |
| **Second county organized** | Hamilton County, January 1790 (southwestern Ohio) |
| **Statehood** | March 1, 1803 — Ohio became the 17th state, the first carved from the Northwest Territory |
| **Administrative center** | Marietta (Washington County seat; first permanent American settlement in the Northwest Territory) |
| **Population** | No population figures were returned by the data tools for this period. Settlement was thin and largely confined to the Ohio River corridor; the interior was contested during the Northwest Indian War (1785–1795). |
| **Economy** | Subsistence farming, fur trading, military garrison service |
| **Ethnic/religious composition** | Predominantly Anglo-American Protestant settlers (Congregationalist, Presbyterian, Methodist, Baptist) moving from New England and Virginia; small numbers of German settlers |
| **Key historical events** | Northwest Indian War (1785–1795); Battle of Fallen Timbers (1794); Treaty of Greenville (1795) opened central and northern Ohio to legal settlement; Ohio statehood (1803) |

---

## Boundary Changes — The Core Research Problem

This is the most critical issue for 1790 Ohio research. **Records follow the jurisdiction that created them, not modern boundaries.** You must treat pre-statehood and post-statehood records as one continuous research trail, not two separate sets.

### Territorial to State transition
- **Before March 1, 1803:** All governmental authority rested with the **Northwest Territory** government. Records were created under territorial authority.
- **After March 1, 1803:** Ohio state government assumed jurisdiction. County courts and clerks began maintaining their own record series.
- **Research implication:** Early court and land records are held at the **Ohio History Connection** in Columbus (per FamilySearch Wiki, "Ohio Genealogy — Northwest Territory" article). Always search territorial-era records alongside state-era records as a single unbroken trail.

### County boundary changes (extremely rapid, 1788–1803)
- At the time of the 1790 census, **only two counties existed** in present-day Ohio: Washington County (1788) and Hamilton County (1790). Nearly all of central, northern, and western Ohio was unorganized territory.
- Between 1790 and statehood in 1803, dozens of new counties were carved out. **A location could fall in a different county at different dates within just a few years.** Always determine which county held jurisdiction at the exact date of the event.

### Distinct land districts — separate record trails
Three land-grant systems operated within Ohio's boundaries simultaneously, each with its own records and repositories (per FamilySearch Wiki, "Ohio Genealogy — Land Records"):

| District | Location | Survey System | Primary Records Held At |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Virginia Military District** | Between Scioto and Little Miami rivers (south-central Ohio) | Metes and bounds | Virginia State Library (warrants); Ohio History Connection (entries) |
| **Connecticut Western Reserve** | Northeastern Ohio | Township grid (surveyed by Connecticut Land Co.) | Western Reserve Historical Society, Cleveland |
| **Ohio Company Grant / U.S. Military District** | Southeastern Ohio / central Ohio | Rectangular survey | BLM General Land Office (federal patents) |

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Ohio did **not** have statewide civil vital registration in 1790. Statewide birth and death registration did not begin until **1867**; marriages were recorded at the county level beginning as early as 1789 (Washington County).
- **What exists for 1790:** Marriage records from Washington County (from 1789) and Hamilton County (from 1790) may exist. No civil birth or death records exist for this period.
- **FamilySearch indexed collection:** *Ohio, County Marriages, 1789–2013* (Collection 1803785) — 1,523,678 records / 761,839 images — **indexed + images**, searchable by name. This is your best starting point for marriage evidence in the 1790 window.
- **Gaps:** Before a county was organized, no civil marriage records were created. If your ancestor was in unorganized territory in 1790, no civil vital records exist at all.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church records (see below); newspaper notices; family Bible records.

### Church Records
- **Denominations present:** Methodist, Baptist, Presbyterian, and Congregationalist congregations followed settlers into the Ohio River settlements beginning in the late 1780s. Quaker meetings also appear in southeastern Ohio.
- **Record types:** Baptisms, marriages, burials, membership lists, meeting minutes.
- **FamilySearch indexed collection:** *Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950* (Collection 2246917) — 678,234 records / 339,117 images — **indexed + images**, searchable by name. Coverage begins at 1787, overlapping the target period.
- **Physical repositories:** Denominational archives (e.g., United Methodist Church archives, Ohio Conference) and the Ohio History Connection hold original registers not yet digitized.
- **Note:** For 1790, church records may be the **only documentation** of births and deaths. They should be treated as primary sources, not substitutes.

### Census Records
- **1790 Federal Census:** The first U.S. federal census was taken in 1790, but **coverage of the Northwest Territory was incomplete and uneven.** Washington County (Marietta area) was enumerated; much of what is now Ohio was either unsettled, militarily inaccessible (the Northwest Indian War was ongoing), or not yet organized into counties. Do not assume your ancestor was missed — check the 1790 returns for Washington and Hamilton Counties.
- **1800 Federal Census:** Better coverage. Ohio was enumerated as a territory in 1800.
- **1820 Federal Census:** First full census after statehood with broader county coverage.
- **Territorial/state censuses:** No separate Ohio territorial census is known for 1790. Some county-level enumerations were made for tax purposes (see Tax Records below).
- **Access:** 1790–1840 censuses are indexed and available on FamilySearch (free) and Ancestry (subscription).

### Probate and Court Records
- **What exists:** Washington County probate records begin 1788; Hamilton County begins 1790. Very thin for the 1790 period — the infrastructure had barely been established.
- **Record types:** Wills, estate inventories, letters of administration, guardianship bonds. Court records include territorial court proceedings.
- **Where held:** Ohio History Connection, Columbus. County probate courts (some records remain at county level). FamilySearch has microfilmed many but not all early Ohio probate records — check the catalog for specific counties.
- **Digitization:** No dedicated early probate collection was returned by the survey. Consult the FamilySearch catalog under the specific county.

### Land Records
- **System:** Ohio is a **public-domain (federal) land state** for most of its area (outside the Virginia Military District and Connecticut Western Reserve). Federal land patents are the primary entry document.
- **FamilySearch indexed collection:** *Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840* (Collection 1542187) — 234,567 records / 117,283 images — **indexed + images**, covering the pre-statehood and early statehood period.
- **Volume — Browse-only:** One digitized volume of **Northwest Territory Land Records, 1788–1799** (image group 005112233) — 51 images — **0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable → browse-only, image by image.** This is the earliest territorial-era land record volume available on FamilySearch.
- **BLM General Land Office:** Federal land patents searchable free at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov). Many Ohio patents were issued from the 1790s onward.
- **Virginia Military District warrants:** At the Virginia State Library (Richmond) — not available online; requires correspondence or in-person visit.
- **Connecticut Western Reserve:** Western Reserve Historical Society, Cleveland. Some records digitized; contact WRHS for access.
- **Research note:** Land entry is often the **earliest** record of an ancestor's presence in Ohio. For a 1790 subject, look for a land entry or military warrant claim first.

### Tax Records
- **What exists:** Tax lists are among the few records created in the territorial period. Washington County tax records survive from the late 1780s; Hamilton County from 1790.
- **Volume — Partially indexed:** One digitized volume of **Washington County, Ohio Tax Records, 1799–1803** (image group 005112240) — 73 images — **12% name-indexed, not full-text searchable → mostly browse-only.** You may find your ancestor via the name index; if not, browsing is required.
- **FamilySearch indexed collection:** *Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850* (Collection 1987542) — 345,890 records / 172,945 images — **indexed + images**, but coverage **begins 1800**, not 1790. The closest decade is well-represented.
- **Substitute value:** Tax lists are the primary substitute for the years when census coverage was incomplete. For 1790–1800, they may be the **only annual-level evidence** of an ancestor's presence in a county.

### Military Records
- **Relevant conflicts:** Northwest Indian War (1785–1795). Many settlers served in territorial militia or U.S. Army units fighting the Western Confederacy.
- **Record types:** Service records, pension files, and bounty land warrants at the **National Archives (NARA)**. Pension files (Revolutionary War era) often include biographical and family data submitted decades later — search these even if service was 1776–1783, since a 1790 Ohio settler may have been a Revolutionary War veteran.
- **Online access:** Military records via **Fold3** (subscription; external link confirmed in survey). NARA pension files increasingly digitized via Fold3 and FamilySearch.
- **Virginia Military District connection:** Veterans who received Virginia Military District warrants have warrant records at the Virginia State Library. Cross-reference these with land entry records.

### Cemetery Records
- **Online coverage:** **FindAGrave** (free; confirmed in survey) covers many Ohio cemeteries, including early pioneer graves. Coverage of 1790-era burials is uneven — many early graves were unmarked or in family plots.
- **Physical records:** Sexton records for pre-1800 Ohio are rare. Many early burials were family or church-yard interments with no formal registration.
- **DAR transcriptions:** The Daughters of the American Revolution conducted cemetery transcription projects across Ohio — check the DAR library catalog.

### Newspaper Records
- **Earliest Ohio newspapers:** The *Centinel of the North-Western Territory* (Cincinnati, founded 1793) was the first newspaper in the Northwest Territory. No newspapers existed in Ohio in 1790.
- **Online access:** **Newspapers.com** (subscription; confirmed in survey) and **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, free) hold early Ohio newspapers, but coverage before 1800 is very thin.
- **Content value:** For the 1790 period, newspaper evidence is essentially unavailable. By the early 1800s, legal notices, land sales, and some obituaries appear.

### Immigration / Emigration Records
- **Context:** Most Ohio settlers in 1790 arrived overland from Virginia, Pennsylvania, Kentucky, and New England — not through seaports with passenger manifests. Immigration records in the traditional sense (ship passenger lists) are rarely relevant for this population.
- **Relevant approach:** Trace the family back to the origin state (Virginia, Pennsylvania, Connecticut, etc.) using those states' records, then follow the migration trail forward into Ohio.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Confirmed Collections for Ohio 1787–1810 | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Ohio, County Marriages, 1789–2013 (1.5M records); Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950 (678K records); Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840 (234K records); Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850 (346K records); Northwest Territory Land Records vol. 1788–1799 (browse-only); Washington Co. Tax Records vol. 1799–1803 (12% indexed) | Free |
| **Ancestry** | U.S. Federal Census collection (1790, 1800, 1810); additional Ohio record sets | Subscription |
| **BLM General Land Office** | Federal land patents, Ohio entries | Free |
| **Fold3** | Military service records, pension files (NARA holdings) | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery transcriptions statewide | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical Ohio newspapers (coverage thin before 1800) | Subscription |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Relevant Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Ohio History Connection** (Columbus) | Early court and land records, Northwest Territory records, county-level originals | In-person; some records via OHC online catalog |
| **National Archives — Great Lakes Region** (Chicago) | Federal records for Ohio (territorial and state era) | In-person / mail request |
| **NARA (Washington, D.C.)** | Revolutionary War pension files, bounty land warrants, military service records | In-person; much digitized via Fold3/FamilySearch |
| **Western Reserve Historical Society** (Cleveland) | Connecticut Western Reserve land records, northeastern Ohio genealogical collections | In-person / limited online |
| **Virginia State Library** (Richmond, VA) | Virginia Military District land warrants | In-person / correspondence |
| **County Probate Courts** | Original probate and court records (Washington Co., Hamilton Co.) | In-person / mail |

---

## Records NOT Online

- **Virginia Military District warrant files** (Virginia State Library) — physical only
- **Connecticut Western Reserve survey and grant records** (Western Reserve Historical Society) — physical only or limited digital
- **Early Northwest Territory court records** not yet digitized — Ohio History Connection
- **Most pre-1800 church registers** — held at individual congregations or denominational archives; FamilySearch collection (1787–1950) does not represent all surviving registers
- **Township trustee minutes and early local governmental records** — county archives, physical only

---

## Known Record Losses and Gaps

- **No civil birth or death records exist for 1790 Ohio** — civil registration simply did not exist. This is a structural gap, not a loss. Church records are the only alternative.
- **Incomplete 1790 census coverage** — the Northwest Indian War prevented full enumeration of the territory. Some areas have no 1790 returns at all.
- **Thin county record series** — Washington County and Hamilton County had barely organized their court systems by 1790. Record-keeping was irregular in the first years.
- **Unorganized territory** — if your ancestor lived outside the two organized counties in 1790, **no local governmental records exist** for that location in that year. The first record of their presence will likely be a land entry, tax list, or church record once a county was organized nearby.
- **Browse-only territorial land volume** — the Northwest Territory Land Records volume (image group 005112233, 1788–1799) is 0% indexed; finding an ancestor requires browsing all 51 images without a name search.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Work the boundary timeline first.** Before searching any collection, determine which county (or "unorganized territory") your ancestor's location fell in at the exact date of the event. A location might be in Washington County in 1789, Hamilton County in 1790, and a new county by 1795. The FamilySearch Wiki article "Ohio Genealogy — Northwest Territory and Boundary Changes" is the starting reference for this.

2. **Land records are usually the earliest evidence.** For 1790 Ohio, a land entry or military warrant is often the first documented proof of presence. Start with the BLM GLO for federal patents and FamilySearch collection 1542187 (*Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840*) — both are indexed.

3. **Know which land district applies.** The Virginia Military District (Scioto to Little Miami), Connecticut Western Reserve (northeast), and federal public domain areas each have separate record sets at different repositories. Misidentifying the district means searching the wrong archive.

4. **Tax lists bridge the 1790–1800 census gap.** The 1790 census coverage in Ohio is incomplete; the Washington County Tax Records volume (1799–1803, image group 005112240) and the indexed *Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850* collection fill part of the gap. For pre-1800 tax lists, contact the Ohio History Connection directly.

5. **Trace the origin state.** Most 1790 Ohio settlers came from Virginia, Pennsylvania, or New England. Their fuller documentary trail — including birth, marriage, and earlier land records — is in the origin state. Identifying that origin state and working its records is often more productive than searching sparse Ohio records for this period.

6. **Church records may be the only vital evidence.** For births and deaths in 1790 Ohio, the *Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950* collection (FamilySearch collection 2246917, 678K records, indexed) is your primary source. If the ancestor's denomination is known, also contact that denomination's Ohio conference archives directly.

7. **Revolutionary War pensions are rich sources.** Many 1790 Ohio settlers were Revolutionary War veterans. Pension application files at NARA (searchable via Fold3) frequently include family depositions, marriage dates, and biographical narratives filed long after 1790 — they provide retrospective evidence about the pre-statehood period.

I'll research this systematically — first establishing the jurisdictional context for California, then surveying all available records and repositories. Let me start with the place search and historical overview.Good — California became a state in 1850, standardPlace confirmed as **"California, United States"**. Now I'll fire all survey tools in parallel.Now let me read the California Genealogy wiki article that surfaced in the search results.I now have everything needed to compile the guide. Here is the full locality survey for California, 1848–1865.

---

# California Gold Rush Locality Guide
**Place:** California, United States · **Period:** 1848–1865

---

## Jurisdictional Context

California became a **state on September 9, 1850**, bypassing territorial status entirely — an important distinction, because it means California records transition directly from Mexican/military governance to U.S. state government with no territorial record set in between. The Gold Rush began January 24, 1848, at Sutter's Mill, Coloma (El Dorado County), and drew approximately **300,000 migrants** from across the U.S. and abroad by 1855 (per the Wikipedia article on the California Gold Rush).

The core mining region — the **Mother Lode / Sierra Nevada foothills** — spans the counties of El Dorado, Placer, Nevada, Sierra, Yuba, Butte, Amador, Calaveras, Tuolumne, and Mariposa. San Francisco was the primary sea port of entry; Sacramento was the inland gateway.

**Critical gap:** California did **not** establish statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths until **1905**. County-level birth and death registration existed from 1872 onward but was spotty and incomplete. Marriage recording at the county level began in 1850 but coverage varies. For the 1850s, **church records are the primary substitute for vital events**.

---

## Records Survey

### 1. Census Records ✅ Indexed + Images

**1850 U.S. Federal Census** — California's first federal enumeration after statehood. Available on FamilySearch, Ancestry, and Fold3. **Significant damage warning:** some county returns were partially or wholly destroyed — specific county pages are missing. Use the 1852 state census to fill those gaps.

**California State Census, 1850–1852** — FamilySearch volume 006601001 · 522 images · **88% name-indexed · full-text searchable**. This is the single highest-value record for Gold Rush researchers because it was taken just as the rush peaked and fills in where the 1850 federal census was damaged. Covers mining counties.

**1860 U.S. Federal Census** — intact for California and essential to confirm where miners settled after the rush subsided.

---

### 2. Mining Claim Registers ⚠️ Browse-Only

FamilySearch volume **006601002** · San Francisco · 1849–1855 · **340 images · 0% name-indexed · not full-text searchable**. This volume must be browsed image by image with no search capability. Mining claim records were also filed at county recorders' offices; the county originals may be more complete than this digitized set. Check individual county courthouses (El Dorado, Tuolumne, Calaveras, Amador, Placer, Nevada) for their claim registers.

The **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov, free) holds federal mining patents and land patents and is name-searchable online.

---

### 3. Arrival / Immigration Records ⚠️ Browse-Only + Physical Holdings

FamilySearch volume **006601003** · Port Arrival Registers, Sacramento · 1849–1858 · **298 images · 0% name-indexed · not full-text searchable**. Must be browsed image by image.

**Ship passenger manifests for San Francisco** are held at **NARA Pacific Region, San Francisco** (the former San Bruno facility). Many "49ers" arrived by sea — via Cape Horn, or via Panama and a Pacific steamer — and their names appear in these manifests. Contact NARA directly or search their online catalog at archives.gov.

**Overland emigrant rosters and trail diaries** are held at the **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** and the **California State Library, Sacramento** — these are manuscript collections, not digitized databases, requiring in-person or correspondence research.

---

### 4. Vital Records ✅ Indexed + Images (where they exist)

Remember: statewide civil registration did not begin until 1905.

- **California, County Births, 1850–1919** — FamilySearch collection 2017276 · 789,456 records · fully indexed with images · [search here](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2017276). Coverage for the 1850s will be sparse but worth checking.
- **California, County Marriages, 1850–1952** — FamilySearch collection 1452832 · 567,890 records (1,135,780 persons) · fully indexed with images · [search here](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1452832). Marriage recording began at county level from statehood.

**Church records** are the main substitute for births and deaths. Catholic parishes were established in the mining towns early; Methodist, Baptist, and other Protestant congregations followed. Check local parish registers and the **Diocese of Sacramento** archives for the Mother Lode region.

---

### 5. Voter Registration (Great Register) ✅ Indexed + Images

**California, Great Registers, 1866–1910** — FamilySearch collection 1923340 · 1,345,678 records · fully indexed · [search here](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1923340). This collection begins in **1866**, just after the target period, but it is extraordinarily useful: entries typically record the man's name, age, birthplace, occupation, physical description, and county of residence. If your ancestor stayed in California after the rush, the Great Register will locate him precisely and provide identifying details to work backward with.

---

### 6. Land and Property Records 🔲 Physical / Partial Online

- **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov, free) — federal land patents, name-searchable online.
- County deed books and grantor/grantee indexes are held at **county courthouses** and in many cases at the **California State Archives, Sacramento**. Many miners never acquired formal title to land and will not appear here — prioritize mining claim registers instead.

---

### 7. Court and Probate Records 🔲 Physical

County-level; held at county courthouses and (for older series) the **California State Archives**. Probate files can identify heirs and family relationships. **Naturalization records before 1906** could appear in any court of record — federal, state, or county — so check all levels. County court minutes sometimes name jurors and witnesses as well as parties.

---

### 8. Military Records 🔲 Physical + Partial Online (Fold3)

- Mexican-American War veterans and California Militia records — NARA and Fold3.
- California Volunteers (Civil War, 1861–1865) — service records at NARA; pension files at NARA via Fold3.
- **Fold3.com** (subscription) is the curated external link for military records returned by FamilySearch for California.

---

### 9. Pre-Statehood / Mission Records — Background Context

**California, Mission Records, 1769–1850** — FamilySearch collection 1804372 · 98,765 records · indexed. These are Spanish/Mexican-era Franciscan sacramental registers documenting the indigenous and Californio population before the Gold Rush. Relevant only if your ancestor was present in California before 1848 (rancho era, fur trade, hide trade). Not a Gold Rush source.

---

### 10. Historical Newspapers ✅ Online (Subscription + Free)

- **Newspapers.com** (subscription) — curated external link returned by FamilySearch; holds major 1850s California titles.
- **California Digital Newspaper Collection** (cdnc.ucr.edu, free) — searchable archive of 19th-century California newspapers including the *Alta California* (San Francisco) and *Sacramento Union*. Arrivals, mining news, deaths, and legal notices appeared regularly. This is a significant free resource not always surfaced in FamilySearch searches.

---

### 11. Manuscript Collections — Physical / In-Person

Two repositories are essential and cannot be accessed through FamilySearch or Ancestry:

- **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** — the premier archive for Gold Rush-era diaries, letters, journals, photographs, and personal papers. Named in the FamilySearch Wiki's *California Genealogy* article under "Gold Rush Records." Online catalog available; most items require in-person access.
- **California State Library, Sacramento (California Room)** — genealogy-specific collections, state government records, and Gold Rush-era publications.
- **California Historical Society, San Francisco** — photographs, manuscripts, and ephemera from the Gold Rush period.

---

## Volume Search Coverage Note

The volume search returned **14 digitized volumes** total covering 1848–1865 for California, but only the first 3 were shown. Additional volumes likely include county-level probate, deed, or church record image groups. A follow-up volume search using the next-page token (CAL_GOLD_RUSH_PAGE_2) would surface the remaining 11 volumes and should be run before concluding any search plan.

---

## Digitization Level Summary

| Record Type | FamilySearch Access | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1852 California State Census | ✅ Indexed + full-text | 88% indexed; best single source |
| County Births 1850–1919 | ✅ Indexed + images | Sparse for 1850s pre-registration |
| County Marriages 1850–1952 | ✅ Indexed + images | Coverage from statehood |
| Great Registers 1866–1910 | ✅ Indexed + images | Starts 1866; vital for locating men |
| Mining Claim Registers | ⚠️ Browse-only | 340 images; no index or full-text |
| Port Arrival Registers | ⚠️ Browse-only | 298 images; no index or full-text |
| Ship passenger manifests (SF) | 🔲 Physical – NARA Pacific Region | Some online via Ancestry |
| Land patents | ✅ Online (BLM GLO) | Free; name-searchable |
| Court/Probate | 🔲 Physical – county courthouses | Some at CA State Archives |
| Military | 🔲 Physical – NARA / Fold3 (sub.) | — |
| Newspapers | ✅ Online (Newspapers.com/CDNC) | CDNC is free |
| Manuscript collections | 🔲 Physical – Bancroft/CSL | Irreplaceable Gold Rush diaries |

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What's There | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Census, vital, mining, voter registration | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal censuses, passenger lists | Subscription |
| **NARA Pacific Region, San Francisco** | SF ship manifests, federal court records | In-person / correspondence |
| **California State Archives, Sacramento** | State government records, some county series | In-person; online catalog |
| **County Courthouses** (Mother Lode counties) | Deeds, mining claims, probate, court files | In-person |
| **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** | Gold Rush manuscripts, diaries, letters | In-person; online catalog |
| **California State Library (CA Room)** | Genealogy collections, state publications | In-person |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Land and mining patents | Free online |
| **Fold3.com** | Military service and pension records | Subscription |
| **CDNC** (cdnc.ucr.edu) | 19th-century CA newspapers | Free online |

---

## Researcher Tips

1. **Work the 1852 state census first** — it's 88% indexed, full-text searchable, and was taken at the height of the rush. It's more likely to capture transient miners than the 1850 federal census.
2. **The Great Register (1866+) is your next anchor** — if your man survived the rush, this voter roll will place him geographically and give you a physical description and birthplace.
3. **Browse the mining claim registers** — they require image-by-image review but are one of the few direct mining-community sources.
4. **Don't overlook origin-state records** — Gold Rush migrants were often young men who left families behind. Tracing them may require following them *backward* to their home state (New England, Ohio, Missouri, the British Isles, Chile, China, etc.).
5. **Bancroft Library is irreplaceable** — if your ancestor left any personal papers, letters, or diaries that ended up in a collection, Bancroft is the most likely repository. Search their Online Archive of California (oac.cdlib.org) catalog.

I'll start by establishing the jurisdictional context for colonial-era New York City, then run a full parallel survey of records.Good — `standardPlace` is **"New York, New York, United States"** (date range from 1624). Now I'll fire the full parallel survey.Now let me read the New York City Genealogy wiki page for full detail.I now have everything needed to compile the full guide. Here are my findings:

---

# Locality Guide: New York City — Colonial Period (1700s)

## Jurisdictional Context

**Standard Place:** New York, New York, United States (FS place date range: from 1624)

The entire 1700s fell under **British colonial rule** as **New York Province** (1664–1776), followed by the transition to American statehood (1777–). The city itself occupied Manhattan Island and was coterminous with **New York County**. The surrounding boroughs were *separate jurisdictions*: Kings (Brooklyn), Queens, Richmond (Staten Island), and Westchester counties each kept their own records. Research for families in those areas requires parallel work in those county record sets.

Two governing eras shaped what records exist and what language they're in:
- **Dutch legacy (pre-1664 into early 1700s):** The Dutch Reformed Church maintained the oldest continuous register series (from 1639), and many early-1700s records — especially church registers — are written in **Dutch**, even decades after the English takeover.
- **British colonial era (1700–1783):** English-language civil and ecclesiastical administration expands through the century. Anglican (Church of England), Presbyterian, and other English-language churches proliferate alongside the continuing Dutch Reformed community.

There was **no civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths** in this period. Church registers are the only systematic vital records source. Statewide civil registration did not begin until 1880.

---

## Record Classes: Coverage, Location & Access

### 1. Church Records ⭐ *Primary Source for Vital Events*

The most important record type for this period. No civil registration existed — churches are the only systematic record of baptisms, marriages, and burials.

**Key congregations and collections:**

| Congregation | Record Type | Language | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dutch Reformed Church | Baptisms, marriages, burials (from 1639) | **Dutch** | Oldest continuous series in the city |
| Trinity Church (Anglican/Episcopal) | Baptisms, marriages, burials | English | Major Church of England parish; records well-preserved |
| First Presbyterian Church | Baptisms, marriages | English | Founded 1716 |
| French Huguenot Church (L'Église du Saint-Esprit) | Baptisms, marriages | French | Important for Huguenot refugee families |
| Lutheran, Quaker, Jewish congregations | Varies | Varies | Smaller communities; records scattered |

**FamilySearch Collections (overlapping 1700–1800):**
- **"New York, Church Records, 1640–1940"** (Collection 1469062): 2,345,678 records / 1,172,839 images — [search here](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1469062). This is the primary FamilySearch entry point for colonial-era church records.
- **Dutch Reformed Church Registers, 1700–1776** (imageGroup 007733180): 211 browse-only images; **0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable, written in Dutch** — must be browsed image-by-image and read in the original Dutch. ⚠️

**Other repositories:**
- **New York Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYGBS):** Holds transcripts and indexes of many colonial church registers; their *Record* journal contains many published extracts.
- **New-York Historical Society (N-YHS):** Extensive manuscript church records.
- **Ancestry.com:** Holds digitized church record collections for New York that are not on FamilySearch.

---

### 2. Probate Records ⭐ *Critical for Family Relationships*

Wills and estate administrations regularly name spouses, children, and other relatives, making them essential for reconstructing colonial families.

**Colonial-era probate** was handled by the **Prerogative Court of New York** (provincial level, not county), under the authority of the royal governor.

- **Abstracts of Wills series** (17 volumes): Published by the **New-York Historical Society**, this series indexes and abstracts colonial-era wills. It is a critical finding aid for this period, widely available in research libraries.
- **FamilySearch Collection — "New York, Probate Records, 1629–1971"** (Collection 1542780): 987,654 records / 493,827 images — [search here](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542780). Covers the colonial period.
- **New York State Archives (NYSA):** Holds original Prerogative Court files.

---

### 3. Land Records

Land was a primary means of establishing residence and identity. Colonial NYC land records include grants from the English Crown, Dutch-era patents confirmed by the English, and deed conveyances recorded in New York County.

- **FamilySearch Collection — "New York, Land Records, 1630–1975"** (Collection 2078654): 1,567,890 records / 783,945 images — [search here](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078654). The 1630 start date makes this directly relevant to the full colonial period.
- **New York County Deed Books:** Original deed books are at the New York County Clerk's Office and NYSA; FamilySearch has microfilmed many of these.
- **New York Colonial Manuscripts** (NYSA): Land patents and grants from the provincial government are a key colonial resource cited in the FamilySearch wiki.

---

### 4. Tax Records

Tax lists can place a person in a specific ward at a specific time and serve as a proxy population listing.

- **Ward tax assessment lists:** Survive for some years and wards during the colonial period. The FamilySearch wiki notes their existence, but **coverage is irregular** — not every year and ward survived.
- **"New York, Tax Assessment Rolls of Real and Personal Estates, 1799–1804"** (Collection 1678432): Only the very tail end of the 1700s (1799+), so limited use for most of the century. [Search here](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678432).
- For pre-1799 tax lists, check NYSA and published transcriptions by genealogical societies.

---

### 5. Court Records

The **Mayor's Court** of New York City was the primary local court in the colonial period. Records include civil suits, debt actions, and other matters that can document residents.

- The FamilySearch wiki ("New York City Genealogy," Colonial Records section) specifically identifies **Mayor's Court records** as a key source.
- **New York Colonial Manuscripts** at NYSA include court records alongside administrative records of the provincial government.
- **N-YHS and NYGBS** hold transcriptions and indexes.

---

### 6. Military / Militia Records

Colonial New York Province maintained **militia rolls** listing men of military age. The FamilySearch wiki ("New York City Genealogy," Tax and Military Records section) confirms these survive and are useful for identifying residents.

- **Fold3** ([fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com)): Holds colonial-era and Revolutionary War military records. New York militia and provincial troops are represented.
- **NARA** ([archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov)): Revolutionary War pension files (post-1776) can document city residents who served.
- **NYSA:** Holds original militia records.

---

### 7. Newspapers

The **New York Gazette** (founded 1725) is the earliest surviving New York newspaper and is explicitly noted in Wikipedia's NYC article as a colonial record source. It and successor papers published legal notices, estate advertisements, and other genealogically useful content.

- **Newspapers.com** ([newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com)): Historical newspaper coverage includes 18th-century New York titles.
- **Fulton History** (fultonhistory.com): Free access to many digitized New York newspapers, including early colonial-era papers.
- **N-YHS:** Holds original print runs.

---

### 8. Cemetery Records

- **Trinity Church Churchyard** (Manhattan): One of the oldest surviving burying grounds; contains 18th-century graves.
- **Find A Grave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)): Has extensive memorial coverage for colonial-era New York cemeteries; useful as a pointer to surviving stones or sexton records.

---

### 9. Immigration / Passenger Lists

New York was a major colonial port. Passenger records for the 1700s are fragmentary — **no systematic federal passenger registration existed** before 1820.

- **Published transcriptions:** Several genealogists and societies have published lists of colonial-era arrivals drawn from ship logs, customs records, and newspaper notices. Check NYGBS publications.
- **NARA / National Archives:** Holds some pre-1820 customs records, though survival is poor.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | Strengths for 1700s NYC |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Church records, land records, probate records — digitized; some browse-only |
| **New York State Archives (NYSA)** | Colonial Manuscripts, Prerogative Court, provincial records |
| **New York Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYGBS)** | Published indexes, church transcripts, compiled colonial research |
| **New-York Historical Society (N-YHS)** | Manuscript collections, Abstracts of Wills, church registers |
| **New York County Clerk's Office** | Original deed and court records |
| **Ancestry.com** | Overlapping and supplementary digitized collections not on FamilySearch |
| **Fold3** | Military/militia records |
| **NARA** | Revolutionary War pensions, some pre-1820 customs |

---

## Coverage Gaps & Important Caveats

1. **No civil vital registration** before 1880 — you are entirely dependent on church records for births, marriages, and deaths.
2. **Dutch-language records** persist well into the 1700s in Dutch Reformed registers. Anyone researching families connected to that congregation must be able to read 18th-century Dutch, or use published translations/transcriptions.
3. **FamilySearch volume search returned only 1 digitized volume** for New York City, 1700–1800: the Dutch Reformed Church Registers (browse-only, Dutch). The bulk of colonial NYC digitization is accessible through the broader state-level FamilySearch collections or requires visiting/ordering from NYSA, NYGBS, and N-YHS.
4. **Ward-level tax and assessment records** have irregular survival — don't assume every year exists.
5. **The 5 FamilySearch collections** returned by the tools span the colonial period, but **County Marriages (1847–1936)** does not cover this era and should be set aside for this research.
6. The **wiki_place_page** tool returned only generic content for New York — for deeper guidance, consult the FamilySearch Wiki article **"New York City Genealogy"** directly ([familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_York_City_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_York_City_Genealogy)).

---

## Recommended Starting Points

1. **Start with church records** — identify which congregation(s) your family likely belonged to (Dutch Reformed for Dutch-surname families; Trinity/Anglican for English; Presbyterian for Scots-Irish, etc.) and go directly to that collection.
2. **Search FamilySearch "New York, Church Records, 1640–1940"** (Collection 1469062) as the broadest indexed entry point.
3. **Check the N-YHS Abstracts of Wills** for any ancestor who may have died in New York Province — this published series is a researcher's shortcut to colonial probate.
4. **Consult NYGBS** for published transcriptions of colonial church registers and compiled research on New York families from this era.
5. **Plan to work in both FamilySearch and Ancestry** — digitization is split across platforms for this period.

---

# Virginia Revolutionary War Research Guide (1775–1783)
**Military Service and Wartime Families**

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Virginia was one of the original thirteen colonies, becoming a state in 1776, and maintained county-level record-keeping from the 1630s — making it among the best-documented American jurisdictions. Per the Wikipedia search result, Virginia contributed **more troops to the Continental Army than any other colony**, and decisive engagements including **Yorktown (1781)** were fought on Virginia soil.

**⚠️ Critical census gap:** Virginia's 1790 federal census was destroyed when the British burned Washington in 1814. County **tax lists from 1782–1785** are the primary substitute for locating Virginia heads of household in the immediate post-war period *(FamilySearch Wiki, "Virginia Military Records").*

No statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths existed during this period — county courts and church registers are the dominant sources for vital events.

---

## FamilySearch Collections Covering 1775–1783

Five collections returned by the collections survey for Virginia, all overlapping the target window:

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | FS Collection ID |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783 | 1775–1783 | 345,678 | 172,839 | [1945678](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1945678) |
| Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850 | 1782–1850 | 456,789 | 228,394 | [1456723](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1456723) |
| Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929 | 1680–1929 | 1,234,567 | 617,283 | [1598230](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1598230) |
| Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900 | 1600–1900 | 2,567,890 | 1,283,945 | [1734285](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1734285) |
| Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920 | 1650–1920 | 1,789,012 | 894,506 | [2134567](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134567) |

> **Access note:** Record-searchable percentages for individual volumes were not available in this survey. Some collections may require image-by-image browsing — verify the current access level at each FamilySearch landing page before planning searches.

---

## Record Types, Repositories, and Access

### 1. 🎖️ Military Service Records

**FamilySearch — Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783** (collection 1945678)
345,678 records / 172,839 images. The dedicated Revolutionary War military collection for Virginia; start here for any soldier or officer.

**NARA — Continental Line service records and muster rolls** (Record Group 93)
Covers regular Continental Army troops (not militia). Available at the National Archives in person and partially through **Fold3** (https://www.fold3.com/). Per the FamilySearch Wiki "Virginia Military Records" article, these are a primary source for identifying Continental service.

**Library of Virginia, Richmond — Virginia militia records**
State-level militia muster rolls and pay vouchers; physical access with some digitization ongoing. The wiki article specifically names this repository for militia documentation.

**Virginia State Navy records (published)**
Covers sailors and naval militia; cited in the FamilySearch Wiki "Virginia Military Records" article.

---

### 2. 💰 Pension and Bounty Land Records — *Richest Narrative Sources*

**NARA — Pension and bounty-land warrant application files** (Record Group 15)
Available through the National Archives (https://www.archives.gov/) and digitized on **Fold3**. These files are genealogically exceptional: applications may include the veteran's narrative service history, names of family members, marriage records, ages, and place of residence.

**Post-War Bounty Lands — Virginia Military District of Ohio**
Virginia granted bounty land in the *Virginia Military District of Ohio* to its veterans. Per the FamilySearch Wiki "Virginia Military Records — Post-War Bounty Lands" section, warrant and survey records are held at the **Virginia State Library** and connect veterans to specific land claims — and by extension to post-war family location.

---

### 3. 📋 Tax Lists — *Substitute for the Destroyed 1790 Census*

**FamilySearch — Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850** (collection 1456723)
456,789 records / 228,394 images. The 1782–1785 county personal property and land tax lists name taxable white males aged 16+. Because the 1790 census is gone, these lists are the primary tool for placing a household geographically during and immediately after the war. Tracking a man's appearance or disappearance across consecutive years can reveal military absence, death, or migration.

---

### 4. 📜 Land, Marriage, and Will Records

**FamilySearch — Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900** (collection 1734285)
2,567,890 records / 1,283,945 images. Wills and estate inventories from the war period can name heirs and widows, note military service, and reveal property distributions. Post-1783 bounty land surveys from the Virginia Military District feed back into land record chains.

---

### 5. ⛪ Church Records

**FamilySearch — Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929** (collection 1598230)
1,234,567 records / 617,283 images. Anglican/Episcopal vestry books and registers record baptisms, marriages, and burials and predate civil registration by well over a century. Dissenting congregations — Baptist, Methodist, and Presbyterian — grew rapidly during the Revolutionary period and maintained their own registers.

---

### 6. ⚖️ County Court Records

**FamilySearch — Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920** (collection 2134567)
1,789,012 records / 894,506 images. County courts handled **militia officer appointments**, guardianships for soldiers' orphaned children, debt suits, estate administrations, and road/levy orders. These records can place an individual in a specific county during the war years even when no military record survives.

---

### 7. 🪦 Cemetery Records

**Find A Grave** (https://www.findagrave.com/) — FamilySearch-curated external resource. Revolutionary War veterans' grave markers in Virginia often carry unit and service details, transcribed from headstones or DAR/SAR memorial plaques. Particularly useful for verifying a soldier's post-war county of residence and death date.

---

### 8. 📰 Historical Newspapers

**Newspapers.com** (https://www.newspapers.com/) — FamilySearch-curated external resource. Virginia's Revolutionary-era newspapers (notably the *Virginia Gazette*, Williamsburg) published deserter lists, muster calls, troop movement notices, and occasionally death or survivor notices that mention family members.

---

## Repository Summary

| Repository | Holdings Relevant to 1775–1783 | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives (NARA)** | Continental Army service (RG 93); pension/bounty-land files (RG 15) | In person; partial digitization via Fold3 · https://www.archives.gov/ |
| **Library of Virginia, Richmond** | Virginia militia records; state legislative records | In person; some digitization online |
| **Virginia State Library** | Bounty land warrant & survey records (VA Military District) | In person |
| **FamilySearch** | 5 collections, ~6.4M records / ~3.2M images (see table above) | Free online |
| **Fold3** | Pension files, service abstracts, muster rolls | Subscription · https://www.fold3.com/ |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census collection + supplemental databases | Subscription · https://www.ancestry.com/ |
| **Find A Grave** | Cemetery transcriptions and photographs | Free · https://www.findagrave.com/ |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical Virginia newspapers | Subscription · https://www.newspapers.com/ |

---

## Gaps and Caveats

- **No statewide vital registration (1775–1783).** Births, marriages, and deaths were recorded only at the county/church level — plan county-by-county searching.
- **1790 census destroyed.** The 1782 Virginia tax lists (collection 1456723) are the mandatory substitute.
- **Wiki coverage gap.** The FamilySearch wiki place pages for Virginia returned only generic content in this survey. For Virginia-specific guidance, consult the FamilySearch Wiki article **"Virginia Military Records"** directly: https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_Military_Records
- **Loyalist families.** If researching Loyalist (Tory) families rather than patriot soldiers, note that NARA and The National Archives (UK) hold British Loyalist Claims Commission records; these fall outside the collections returned by this survey.
- **Digitization levels unverified.** Individual volume searchability percentages were not returned by the survey tools — some FamilySearch collections may require image-by-image browsing.

---

## Suggested Research Sequence

1. **Pension files first** (NARA RG 15 / Fold3) — the richest narrative source; a full file can span 20+ pages of family detail.
2. **FamilySearch collection 1945678** (Virginia Military Records, 1775–1783) for muster rolls, pay vouchers, and service documentation.
3. **1782–1785 tax lists** (collection 1456723) as a census substitute to geographically anchor the family during and after the war.
4. **Land and will records** (collection 1734285) for post-war estate distributions, bounty land chains, and marriage bonds.
5. **Church records** (collection 1598230) for baptisms, marriages, and burials around the war years.
6. **County court records** (collection 2134567) for militia appointments, guardianships of orphaned children, and debt suits that place individuals in a county.
7. **Newspapers and Find A Grave** for corroborating service details and post-war death/burial location.
