# 📍 Locality Guide: New Orleans, Orleans Parish, Louisiana — Early 1800s (c. 1800–1850)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

New Orleans was founded by the French in 1718, passed to Spanish control in 1763, briefly reverted to France, and was acquired by the United States via the **Louisiana Purchase in 1803**. It became the seat of the **Territory of Orleans** (1803–1812) and then **Orleans Parish, State of Louisiana** from 1812. Louisiana uses **parishes** instead of counties and operates under **Napoleonic/civil law** rather than English common law — a distinction that shapes every record type you will encounter. Legal records here include *successions* (probate), *tutorships* (guardianship), and *interdictions*, none of which have direct equivalents in other U.S. states.

---

## Disasters and Their Impact on Records

The FamilySearch Wiki article **"New Orleans Genealogy"** (sourced from `wiki_search` results) identifies the following catastrophic events and their effects:

### Pre-Period Fires (Damage to Colonial-Era Holdings)
| Event | Date | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Good Friday Fire** | 1788 | Destroyed most of the French Quarter and many colonial records |
| **Tallapouse Street Fire** | 1794 | Destroyed approximately 200 more buildings and additional records |

These fires occurred *before* the early 1800s period you are researching, but they explain why **records from the French (1718–1763) and early Spanish (1763–1788) periods are fragmentary**. If your research touches any pre-1788 ancestry, the losses are severe. For events after 1800, the record landscape is substantially better.

### Ongoing Hazards During the Early 1800s
- **Yellow fever epidemics** — frequent and devastating throughout the early 1800s; ironically, the resulting high mortality created demand for church burial records that *do* survive
- **Hurricanes and floods** — repeatedly damaged archival holdings at the local level
- **Jurisdictional transitions** — the handover from Spanish to French to U.S. administration (1800–1803) created gaps in continuity of civil record-keeping; American officials were slow to implement new systems

---

## What Survived — and Where to Find It

### 1. Church Sacramental Registers (Vital Records Substitute) ✅
**What survived:** The **St. Louis Cathedral** sacramental registers — baptisms, marriages, and burials — survive from **1731**, though with gaps caused by the 1788 and 1794 fires. These are the **single most important source** for early 1800s vital events in New Orleans, because Louisiana had no civil registration of births or marriages during this period.

**FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925** (Collection ID 1612905) — **~987,654 records, ~493,827 images** — indexed and searchable at [FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905). This is your starting point for baptisms, marriages, and burials.

**Access note:** The registers were kept in **French** through much of the early 1800s (and sometimes Latin for sacramental formulas). Researchers must be able to read French-language ecclesiastical handwriting or use paleography aids.

**Repository for originals:** The **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** holds the originals; microfilm and digital copies are available via FamilySearch.

---

### 2. Notarial Records ✅ (Browse-Only)
**What survived:** The **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** holds over **30 million pages of notarial acts dating to 1734**. In Louisiana's civil law system, notaries recorded virtually every significant legal transaction — property sales, contracts, successions, manumissions of enslaved persons, business agreements, marriage contracts, and more. This makes notarial records a *uniquely powerful* substitute for many record types that simply don't exist in other states.

**FamilySearch digitization:** `volume_search` returned **1 digitized volume** (image group 004990115) covering **Orleans Parish notarial acts, 1803–1820** — 167 images, **0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable, in French and English**. ⚠️ **Browse-only: must be examined image by image.** No name search is possible through FamilySearch's interface.

**Physical repository:** The **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** (https://www.notarialarchives.org) holds the complete collection and offers in-person and remote research services. This is an essential stop for any New Orleans early 1800s research.

---

### 3. Probate Records (Successions) ✅
**What survived:** Louisiana probate records — called *successions* — survive from the colonial period. They are especially rich because civil law requires an inventory of the estate, often naming heirs, creditors, and (in this period) enslaved individuals.

**FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984** (Collection ID 2078341) — **~456,789 records, ~228,394 images** — available at [FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341). This covers your entire target period.

**Repository for originals:** **Orleans Parish Civil District Court** and the **Louisiana State Archives** in Baton Rouge.

---

### 4. Parish Marriages (Civil) — Partial Coverage Only ⚠️
**FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957** (Collection ID 1798054) — ~567,890 records. This collection **begins in 1837**, so it covers only the *later* part of your early 1800s window. For marriages before 1837, the church sacramental registers (above) are the primary source.

---

### 5. Death Index — Index Only, No Images ⚠️
**FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964** (Collection ID 1543892) — **~2,345,678 index entries, zero images**. This is an index without accompanying death certificates for the early period — the certificates themselves were not systematically kept at the state level until much later. Use this to confirm a name and date, then look for the underlying church burial register.

---

### 6. Census Records ✅
- **U.S. Federal Census** — Orleans Parish appears in **1810, 1820, 1830, 1840** (all within your period). Available on Ancestry.com and through NARA. The 1810 and 1820 censuses name only the head of household; other family members are counted by age/sex category.
- **Spanish Colonial Census** — Pre-1803 censuses (*padrones*) were conducted under Spanish administration; some survive and are held at the Louisiana State Archives and the *Archivo General de Indias* (Seville, Spain) for the Spanish colonial period.

---

### 7. Land Records ✅
In a federal land state like Louisiana, **land grants and patents** issued after 1803 are held at the **Bureau of Land Management's General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) — free and searchable online. Pre-1803 land grants issued under French or Spanish authority require separate research in colonial land records at the Louisiana State Archives.

---

### 8. Military Records ✅
The **War of 1812** and the **Battle of New Orleans (1815)** mean many New Orleans ancestors served in this period. Service records and pension files for this era are at **NARA** (fold3.com carries digitized copies). The **Louisiana State Archives** also holds state militia records.

---

### 9. Newspapers ✅
New Orleans had active newspapers by the early 1800s (e.g., the *Louisiana Gazette*, *Le Moniteur de la Louisiane*). Obituaries, marriage announcements, sheriff's sales, and legal notices appeared regularly. **Newspapers.com** and the **Louisiana Digital Library** (ladigitallibrary.org) hold digitized runs; the **Historic New Orleans Collection** holds additional newspaper archives.

---

### 10. Immigration / Passenger Lists ✅
New Orleans was the **second-largest port of entry** in the United States in the early 1800s. NARA holds passenger arrival lists beginning in 1820 (Customs passenger lists). **Ancestry.com** has these indexed. Pre-1820 arrivals are far less documented.

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | 30M+ pages of notarial acts from 1734 | In-person & remote; notarialarchives.org |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** | St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers | FamilySearch microfilm/digital; in-person |
| **Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge)** | Territorial & early state records, Spanish-era colonial records | In-person; some digitized |
| **Orleans Parish Civil District Court** | Successions (probate), civil court files | In-person |
| **NARA / Ancestry / Fold3** | Federal census, passenger lists, military records | Online |
| **Historic New Orleans Collection** | Newspapers, maps, photographs, manuscripts | In-person & online (hnoc.org) |
| **Archivo General de Indias (Seville, Spain)** | Spanish colonial administrative records | Online partial; in-person |

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Type | FamilySearch Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Church records (St. Louis Cathedral) | **Indexed + images** | Collection 1612905; French-language |
| Notarial Acts 1803–1820 | **Browse-only images** | 167 images; 0% indexed; French/English |
| Probate (Successions) | **Indexed + images** | Collection 2078341 |
| Parish Marriages (civil) | **Indexed + images** | Starts 1837 only |
| Statewide Death Index | **Index only, no images** | Collection 1543892; starts 1819 |
| Federal census 1810–1840 | Indexed + images | Via Ancestry / FamilySearch |
| Notarial acts post-1820 | **Physical / microfilm only** | Visit Notarial Archives Research Center |
| Spanish colonial padrones | **Physical only** | Louisiana State Archives / Seville |

---

## Summary: The Disaster-Survival Picture

The 1788 and 1794 fires hit hardest on **pre-1800 French and early Spanish colonial records**. For the early 1800s specifically, the news is considerably better:

- **Church registers** from St. Louis Cathedral are the backbone of vital event research and substantially survive, filling the gap left by the absence of civil registration.
- **Notarial records** — unique to Louisiana's civil law system — survived in remarkable volume (30M+ pages) and substitute for many record types that simply weren't created here.
- **Probate (succession) records** cover the full period and are indexed and imaged on FamilySearch.
- **The main access challenge** is not destruction but **language** (French, Spanish, and Latin records) and **browse-only digitization** — the notarial acts on FamilySearch require image-by-image examination with no name search.

For records that were destroyed or were never created, the substitution chain is: **church registers → notarial acts → successions → census → newspapers → military records**, roughly in that order of priority.

> ⚠️ **Coverage gap to note:** The FamilySearch wiki place pages for Louisiana returned only generic national content rather than Louisiana-specific guidance. For the full Louisiana research guide, consult **[FamilySearch Wiki: Louisiana Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Louisiana_Genealogy)** and **[FamilySearch Wiki: New Orleans Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_Orleans_Genealogy)** directly — both pages contain detail beyond what the pre-crawled corpus returned here.

Now let me run the full parallel survey.Now let me read the Massachusetts Immigration wiki page and run a broader volume search for the state level.I have everything I need. ---

# Port of Boston Immigration, 1840s — Locality Research Guide

**Place:** Boston, Suffolk, Massachusetts, United States
**Target period:** 1840–1850 (survey extended to 1820–1870 for context and collection continuity)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Boston has been a city since 1630 and was the county seat of Suffolk County throughout the 1840s — no jurisdictional boundary changes affect this record set. Federal customs authority governed all arriving passengers; the US Customs Service created arrival records that are now held in the National Archives.

The 1840s was Boston's heaviest decade of immigration. According to Wikipedia's "Boston" article, the Irish Famine (1845–1852) drove mass emigration to Boston, and by 1850 more than a quarter of the city's residents were Irish-born. German and other European immigrants arrived in smaller but significant numbers during the same years. Boston was one of five major US ports of entry in this period, per the FamilySearch Wiki article "Massachusetts Immigration."

---

## 1. Arrival Records — The Foundation

### Customs Passenger Lists, 1820–1891

This is the **primary record** for tracing port arrivals.

- **What they contain:** Per the FamilySearch Wiki ("Massachusetts Immigration, Port of Boston"), 1840s lists typically record only **name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin**. They do not routinely give a precise town of origin, shipboard family relationships, or a US destination address. This is a critical limitation compared with post-1895 manifests.
- **Original repository:** National Archives and Records Administration, **Record Group 36**. The originals are physically held at the **National Archives at Boston** (located in Waltham, MA).
- **FamilySearch indexed collection:** [Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1784312) — Collection ID 1784312; name-searchable online for free.
- **Underlying digitized image volume:** `volume_search` returned **one volume** (imageGroupNumber 003345210) covering Boston, Suffolk, Massachusetts passenger lists for 1840–1850 — **389 images**, **3% name-indexed**, **full-text searchable (OCR)**. This means only a small fraction of arrivals are reachable via the name index alone; most require either browsing image by image or using full-text OCR search. ⚠️ **Use both the indexed collection (1784312) and full-text search on volume 003345210 to avoid missing your ancestor.**
- **Also on Ancestry.com:** FamilySearch's external links survey returned Ancestry as a curated resource for this place and period. Ancestry holds parallel passenger list holdings and may have entries indexed under different name spellings.

### *The Pilot* "Missing Persons" Advertisements

The FamilySearch Wiki ("Massachusetts Immigration") and Wikipedia's "Boston" article both highlight *The Pilot*, Boston's Irish Catholic newspaper, which published **"Missing Persons" columns** — notices placed by newly arrived immigrants searching for relatives who had come before them. These ads frequently provide **more specific detail than the customs lists**: county of origin in Ireland, ship name, and approximate arrival year. These are searchable through Boston College's online database and through IrishRoots.com, and on microfilm at the Boston Public Library.

---

## 2. Naturalization Records

**FamilySearch collection:** [Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1459213) — Collection ID 1459213; covers the entire 1840s.

⚠️ **Critical caveat:** Before 1906, naturalizations could be filed in **any court of record** — federal, state, or local. For 1840s Boston arrivals, check:
- Suffolk County Court of Common Pleas (local)
- US Circuit Court and US District Court (federal — NARA)
- Massachusetts State Archives (state court filings)

The **Declaration of Intention** ("first papers") and **Petition for Naturalization** are separate filings, often separated by years. Both may survive and both can document country and even town of origin — sometimes more specifically than the passenger list.

---

## 3. Church / Parish Records

For Irish Catholic immigrants — the dominant group — **Catholic parish registers are essential**. Massachusetts civil vital registration began in 1842, but early compliance was uneven, meaning church records may be the only evidence of births, marriages, and deaths in the first years after arrival.

- **FamilySearch Wiki** ("Massachusetts Church Records, Boston Area Churches") identifies the **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** as the repository for the **Cathedral of the Holy Cross** and **St. Patrick's** parish registers, documenting baptisms, marriages, and burials from the 1840s onward.
- **FamilySearch collection:** [Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921657) — Collection ID 1921657; name-indexed.
- For German immigrants: German Lutheran and German Catholic congregations also kept registers. Contact individual denominational archives for access.

---

## 4. Massachusetts Vital Records

**FamilySearch collection:** [Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2061550) — Collection ID 2061550; name-indexed. State civil registration began 1842; coverage improves through the decade. The published *Massachusetts Vital Records to 1850* series (widely available in libraries and on FamilySearch) is a reliable substitute for pre-registration events.

---

## 5. Census Records

- **1840 federal census:** Names head of household only; all other members appear as tick-marks by age and sex. Limited use for tracing individuals but confirms a household in Boston.
- **1850 federal census:** ⭐ First census to name every household member with age, sex, birthplace, and occupation. This is the essential post-arrival snapshot and should be searched immediately after identifying a passenger list arrival.
- Both are available free on FamilySearch and via Ancestry.com.

---

## 6. Probate Records

**FamilySearch collection:** [Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678901) — Collection ID 1678901; covers Suffolk County. Estate files can be surprisingly productive for immigrants: wills and inventories sometimes name relatives still in the home country, and powers of attorney to family members abroad appear in related court files.

---

## 7. Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | Records Held | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives at Boston** (Waltham, MA) | Customs passenger lists (RG 36), federal court naturalizations | In-person; microfilm also at other NARA facilities |
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Passenger lists, naturalization, vital, church, probate collections | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Parallel passenger list holdings, census, newspapers | Subscription |
| **Massachusetts State Archives** (Boston) | State & local court naturalizations, early vital records | In-person; many records on FamilySearch |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** | Catholic parish registers, 1840s onward | Contact archives directly |
| **Boston Public Library** | *The Pilot* on microfilm; historical newspapers | In-person; some digital |
| **New England Historic Genealogical Society** (NEHGS) | Manuscript collections, published vital records, Irish research materials | Membership / reading room |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical newspapers including *The Pilot* | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military records (for immigrants who served) | Subscription |

---

## Key Limitations to Keep in Mind

1. **Thin information on 1840s lists.** Country of origin only — not county or town. The list alone rarely breaks through the "brick wall" back to the home parish.
2. **Low name-indexing in image volume.** Volume 003345210 is only 3% name-indexed; most arrivals must be found through full-text OCR search or browsed image by image.
3. **Pre-1906 naturalization dispersal.** No single index covers all courts — you must check federal, state, and local courts separately.
4. **The Pilot is often more informative than the passenger list** for Irish immigrants; search it in parallel, not as a fallback.

---

## Recommended Search Sequence

1. **Customs passenger list** — FamilySearch collection 1784312; if not found, full-text search / browse volume 003345210 and check Ancestry.
2. ***The Pilot* missing-persons ads** — for Irish immigrants; often supplies the Irish county or townland.
3. **1850 federal census** — confirms presence, household, and birthplace.
4. **Naturalization records** — FamilySearch collection 1459213, then individual court record searches.
5. **Catholic / church parish registers** — Archdiocese of Boston Archives + FamilySearch collection 1921657.
6. **Massachusetts vital records** — collection 2061550 for births, marriages, and deaths after arrival.

Let me compile the full locality guide.

---

# Locality Guide: Native American Ancestors in Oklahoma / Indian Territory, Mid-1800s

---

## 1. Jurisdictional Context

**Oklahoma did not exist as a state during the mid-1800s.** The region was designated **Indian Territory** by the federal government following the Indian Removal Act of 1830. Records created in this period are therefore federal, tribal, or missionary in nature — *not* state or county records. Oklahoma achieved statehood only in 1907.

The dominant nations in Indian Territory during the mid-1800s were the **Five Civilized Tribes** (Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek/Muscogee, and Seminole), forcibly relocated from the American Southeast via the Trail of Tears (late 1830s–early 1840s). Other nations — Osage, Quapaw, Pawnee, Comanche, and others — occupied adjacent lands under separate treaty arrangements.

**Jurisdictional authority** over the Territory passed through:
- **U.S. War Department** → Bureau of Indian Affairs (pre-1849)
- **U.S. Department of the Interior** → Bureau of Indian Affairs (after 1849)
- The **Five Civilized Tribes' own national governments** (with their own courts, citizenship rolls, and laws)

This means the research trail runs through **federal archives (NARA)**, **tribal nation archives**, and **missionary society archives** — not through a state capital or county courthouse.

---

## 2. Record Survey

### 🔴 TIER 1 — Primary Sources for the Mid-1800s Target Period

---

#### Tribal Enrollment Rolls (Pre-Dawes)
**Coverage:** 1830s–1890s | **Access: Indexed + images available**

These are the most direct records for establishing tribal membership during the mid-1800s. Key rolls include:

| Roll | Date | Tribe | Notes |
|------|------|-------|-------|
| Old Settler Cherokee Roll | 1851 | Cherokee | Lists Cherokees who migrated west before the forced removal |
| Drennen Roll | 1852 | Cherokee | Post-removal citizenship enumeration |
| Chapman Roll | 1851 | Cherokee | Eastern Cherokees |
| Various Creek/Choctaw/Chickasaw/Seminole rolls | 1830s–1860s | Multiple nations | Filed with BIA, now at NARA |

FamilySearch holds **"Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959"** (collection 1987432) — 567,890 records with 283,945 images, covering the target period. This is a strong starting point.

FamilySearch also has a digitized volume of **Tribal Enrollment Cards, 1840–1870**, covering Indian Territory: **940 images, 99% name-indexed and full-text searchable** (imageGroupNumber 002298871). This is one of the best-digitized resources for this exact window.

**⚠️ Note on Dawes Rolls (1898–1914):** The Dawes Rolls are the *most widely used* tribal enrollment records for the Five Civilized Tribes and are indexed on both FamilySearch and Ancestry — but they fall **after your target period**. They are essential as a *backward bridge*: a person enrolled in the Dawes process will have stated age, parents, and blood quantum that lets you calculate back to mid-1800s birth/parentage. Always consult Dawes records even when researching the 1840s–1860s.

---

#### Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) Agency Records
**Coverage:** 1820s–1900s | **Access: Physical at NARA; some microfilm + partial online**

The BIA maintained the most voluminous records of Native life in Indian Territory. These include:

- **Annuity payment rolls** — annual lists of tribal members receiving treaty payments; often the closest thing to a census for years between formal enumerations
- **Agency correspondence** — letters between tribal agents and Washington, often naming individuals in disputes, land matters, or family situations
- **School records** — enrollment registers for BIA-run and mission schools
- **Census schedules** — BIA-conducted tribal censuses (sometimes annual for some nations)

**Repository:** Most are at **NARA's Southwest Region in Fort Worth, Texas** (Record Group 75 — Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs). Some have been microfilmed by NARA and are available through FamilySearch library requests or the NARA microfilm catalog. NARA's catalog at [archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov) is the essential index.

---

#### Treaty Documents
**Coverage:** 1830s–1866 | **Access: Published + digitized**

Treaties define which tribes held land in Indian Territory and when, and they often contain schedules of tribal leaders, signatories, or population counts. Key treaties:

- **Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830)** — Choctaw removal
- **Treaty of New Echota (1835)** — Cherokee removal
- **Fort Gibson treaties (1833)** — Creek, Seminole
- **Reconstruction Treaties (1866)** — post-Civil War agreements with all Five Tribes, including provisions for freedmen enrollment

Treaties are published in **Charles Kappler's *Indian Affairs: Laws and Treaties*** (1904), fully digitized and searchable online. Population schedules and signatories in these documents can anchor a family to a specific nation and era.

---

### 🟡 TIER 2 — Supplementary Sources Overlapping the Target Period

---

#### Missionary and Church Records
**Coverage:** 1820s–1900s | **Access: Indexed + images (FamilySearch); physical at denominational archives**

Christian missionaries were active in Indian Territory from the 1820s onward. Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian missionaries kept registers of converts, students, marriages, and burials — often the only surviving record of vital events before the Civil War.

FamilySearch holds **"Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965"** (collection 1654321) — 123,456 records with 61,728 images. This collection directly overlaps the mid-1800s.

**Physical holdings** at denominational archives (American Baptist Historical Society, Presbyterian Historical Society, United Methodist Archives) may contain materials not yet digitized.

---

#### Early Land Records
**Coverage:** 1834–1907 | **Access: Indexed + images (FamilySearch)**

FamilySearch holds **"Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907"** (collection 1541872) — 234,567 records with 117,283 images. Land assignments in Indian Territory were handled differently from U.S. public-domain states: tribal nations held land communally until allotment began under the Dawes Act (1887). However, individual land assignments, improvements, and lease records from the mid-1800s do exist within tribal records and BIA agency files.

For federal allotment patents (post-Dawes), the **BLM General Land Office** website (glorecords.blm.gov) holds searchable records.

---

#### Federal Census Records
**Coverage:** 1860, 1870, 1880 | **Access: Indexed + images (FamilySearch, Ancestry)**

Standard U.S. federal censuses *did* enumerate some residents of Indian Territory, but coverage is **incomplete and inconsistent** for the mid-1800s:

- **1860 census**: Indian Territory was only partially enumerated; many tribal citizens were not included
- **1870 census**: Some Indian Territory schedules survive; coverage varies by nation
- **1880 census**: More systematic enumeration, including some tribal members

These are available at Ancestry.com (full U.S. Federal Census Collection) and FamilySearch. Do not treat absence from a census as evidence of nonexistence — the record gap is the more common explanation.

The **BIA conducted its own tribal censuses** separately from the federal decennial census, often annually; these are frequently more complete for tribal members than the federal schedules.

---

#### Military Records
**Coverage:** 1861–1865 (Civil War) and earlier | **Access: Indexed + images (Fold3, NARA)**

Many tribal members served in the Civil War on both sides. The Five Civilized Tribes were deeply divided, and Confederate Indian units as well as Union Indian Home Guard regiments are documented. Service records and pension files are at NARA and indexed on **Fold3.com** (military records — Fold3 is a curated external resource for this jurisdiction).

Pre-Civil War: some tribal members appear in federal military records related to removal operations or frontier service.

---

#### Cemetery and Burial Records
**Coverage:** Ongoing | **Access: Partial online (FindAGrave)**

**FindAGrave.com** has indexed many Oklahoma/Indian Territory cemeteries, including tribal burial grounds. Coverage is uneven — rural and reservation cemeteries are less likely to be indexed. Death dates from tombstone inscriptions can anchor a mid-1800s birth, and tribal affiliation is sometimes noted.

---

#### Historical Newspapers
**Coverage:** 1840s onward | **Access: Partially online (Newspapers.com)**

Tribal nations published their own newspapers in Indian Territory. The *Cherokee Phoenix* (1828–1834) and its successor the *Cherokee Advocate* (1844–1906) are particularly important — they published legal notices, death notices, and land transactions involving tribal citizens. **Newspapers.com** has some holdings; the **Oklahoma Historical Society** holds additional physical runs.

---

## 3. Key Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **NARA Southwest Region, Fort Worth, TX** | RG 75 — BIA records for Five Civilized Tribes, Dawes enrollment cards, agency files | In-person or microfilm order; some digitized via Fold3/Ancestry |
| **FamilySearch** | Indian Censuses & Rolls (1851–1959), Mission/Church Records (1820–1965), Early Land Records (1834–1907), Tribal Enrollment Cards vol. (1840–1870) | Free online at familysearch.org |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society, Oklahoma City** | Tribal newspapers, BIA microfilm, territorial court records, photo archives | In-person; OHS online catalog at okhistory.org |
| **Cherokee Nation Archives, Tahlequah, OK** | Cherokee citizenship records, council acts, court records, tribal newspapers | In-person; some records shared with NARA |
| **Choctaw Nation Archives** | Choctaw citizenship records, council records | Contact the nation directly |
| **Chickasaw Nation Archives** | Chickasaw records including pre-removal documents | Contact the nation directly |
| **Creek (Muscogee) Nation Archives** | Creek national records | Contact the nation directly |
| **Seminole Nation of Oklahoma** | Seminole tribal records | Contact the nation directly |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Censuses (1860–1880), Dawes Rolls, some BIA records | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Military service records, pension files, some BIA records | Subscription |
| **BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov)** | Federal land patents (post-allotment) | Free online |

---

## 4. Research Strategy Notes

**Start with the tribe, not the county.** Because Indian Territory had no county government structure in the mid-1800s, standard county-by-county searching will fail. Identify which tribal nation your ancestor belonged to first — then work through that nation's records.

**Work backward from the Dawes Rolls.** Even though Dawes enrollment ran 1898–1914, applicants stated birth years, parents' names, and blood degree. A Dawes card pointing to a birth in the 1840s or 1850s is a primary bridge into the mid-1800s record world.

**Pre-removal records matter.** If your ancestor was born before removal (pre-1838 for most nations), records may exist in the *origin* states — Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, Tennessee, North Carolina — not in Oklahoma at all. Church records, state censuses, and early federal censuses from those states can document a family before the Trail of Tears.

**Blood quantum and tribal membership are distinct genealogical questions.** Tribal rolls establish *citizenship* in a nation's eyes; blood quantum calculations in later records may reflect political decisions as much as biology. Treat both as evidence to be evaluated, not as definitive biological fact.

**The Civil War disrupted records.** Many tribal records were destroyed or scattered between 1861–1865 because the Five Civilized Tribes were caught in the war. Gaps in BIA correspondence and tribal records for this window are common and expected.

---

## 5. Digitization Summary

| Record Class | Digitization Level | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Tribal enrollment rolls (pre-Dawes) | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch collection 1987432; 1840–1870 vol. 99% indexed |
| BIA agency records (annuity, correspondence) | **Microfilm / physical only** (mostly) | NARA Fort Worth RG 75; some on Fold3 |
| Treaty documents | **Fully digitized + searchable** | Kappler's *Indian Affairs* online |
| Mission / church records | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch collection 1654321 |
| Early land records | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch collection 1541872 |
| Federal census (1860–1880) | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch, Ancestry; Indian Territory coverage incomplete |
| Military records (Civil War) | **Indexed + images** | Fold3 |
| Tribal newspapers | **Partial** | Some on OHS; some on Newspapers.com |
| Tribal nation archives | **Physical** | Contact each nation directly |

---

## 6. Suggested Next Steps

1. **Identify the tribal nation** your ancestor belonged to before searching any specific roll.
2. **Search FamilySearch collection 1987432** (Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls) and the **1840–1870 Tribal Enrollment Cards volume** (imageGroupNumber 002298871 — 99% indexed, full-text searchable).
3. **Search the Dawes Rolls** on FamilySearch or Ancestry and work backward from stated ages and parents.
4. **Order BIA agency records from NARA Fort Worth** using RG 75 finding aids if FamilySearch holdings don't resolve the question.
5. **Contact the relevant tribal nation's archive** — nations hold records that were never transferred to NARA.
6. If the ancestor was born pre-removal, **shift to origin-state records** (Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, North Carolina, Tennessee) for that generation.

Would you like me to build a full **research plan** targeting a specific person or tribal nation, or help you search any of these collections directly?

# Pennsylvania Locality Guide — Quaker Research, c. 1770–1800

## Jurisdictional Context

Pennsylvania was founded in 1682 by William Penn as a Quaker haven, and by the late 1700s the Religious Society of Friends was deeply established — particularly in **Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware counties**. Pennsylvania ratified the U.S. Constitution in 1787. **Crucially, Pennsylvania did not mandate statewide civil vital registration until 1906**, meaning Quaker meeting records are not supplemental sources — for this period they *are* the vital records. The 1770–1800 window spans the Revolutionary War era, which is genealogically significant because many Quakers who served in the military were formally disowned by their meetings, generating disciplinary records that are themselves valuable genealogical evidence.

---

## ⭐ Quaker Meeting Records — The Primary Source

Quaker monthly meetings kept some of the most systematic records of any religious organization in colonial and early national America. According to the FamilySearch Wiki article *Pennsylvania Church Records*, monthly meetings recorded:

| Record Type | Genealogical Value |
|---|---|
| **Births/Naming records** | Dates and parents' names; substitute for baptism registers |
| **Marriage certificates** | Signed by *all attendees* — a goldmine for identifying relatives and neighbors |
| **Death / burial records** | Name, date, sometimes age |
| **Certificates of Removal** | Record a family moving *from* one meeting *to* another — documents migration |
| **Disownment (disciplinary) records** | Name individuals who violated Quaker discipline (military service, marrying outside the faith, etc.) — often give family context |
| **Monthly/Quarterly/Yearly meeting minutes** | Broader organizational records that name members |

### Structure of Quaker Meetings in Pennsylvania

Meetings were organized hierarchically. **Monthly meetings** (the basic unit) fed into **quarterly meetings**, which fed into the **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting** — the governing body for Pennsylvania and the mid-Atlantic. Knowing which monthly meeting your ancestors belonged to is essential for locating the right register.

---

## Repositories and Access

### 1. Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College
**The single largest repository of American Quaker records.** Per the FamilySearch Wiki and Wikipedia, this library holds original meeting registers, minutes, and certificates for Pennsylvania monthly meetings. Many have been microfilmed by FamilySearch.
- 📍 Swarthmore College, Swarthmore, PA
- 🌐 [www.swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library](https://www.swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library)
- Access: In-person and some digitized holdings; contact in advance

### 2. Quaker and Special Collections, Haverford College
A second major repository, particularly strong for meeting records from the Philadelphia region.
- 📍 Haverford College, Haverford, PA
- 🌐 [www.haverford.edu/quaker-special-collections](https://www.haverford.edu/quaker-special-collections)

### 3. FamilySearch — Digitized Holdings

**FamilySearch Volume Search returned one Quaker-specific digitized volume for 1750–1800:**

| Volume | Image Group | Images | Indexed? | Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes, 1750–1800 | 001188322 | 145 | ❌ 0% — browse-only | Images only, no name search |

> ⚠️ **Browse-only warning:** This volume must be browsed image by image with no name search capability. Plan time accordingly.

**Indexed FamilySearch collections overlapping 1770–1800:**

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | Access Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638) | 1708–1985 | 1,245,830 | 607,220 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| [Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957) | 1677–1950 | 312,045 | 156,023 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| [Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909) | 1700–1900 | 198,765 | None | ⚠️ Index only — no images |
| [Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683) | 1683–1994 | 893,214 | 446,607 | ✅ Indexed + images |

> **Note on the Church and Town Records collection:** This is the primary FamilySearch collection likely to contain Quaker meeting register entries alongside records from other denominations. Search it with the ancestor's name *and* filter by county.

### 4. Published Abstract — Hinshaw's Encyclopedia
**William Wade Hinshaw, *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, 6 volumes** — abstracts entries from Pennsylvania monthly meeting records. This is a heavily used finding aid, and many libraries hold physical copies. FamilySearch has microfilmed the underlying records that Hinshaw abstracted. Search Hinshaw before investing time browsing original volumes; it may give you the meeting name and approximate date range you need.

---

## Supporting Record Types for the Period

### Land Records
Pennsylvania is a **state-land (warrant) state** — not a federal land state. Land grants were issued by the Pennsylvania proprietors (and later the Commonwealth). These records are held at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** in Harrisburg and at **county deed books** (county courthouses). FamilySearch has microfilmed many county deed books. Land records in this era can establish neighbors (some of whom may be kin), approximate wealth, and place of residence when no vital record exists.

### Probate Records
The indexed [Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683) collection (893,214 records, 446,607 images) is an excellent supplement. Wills from this period name heirs, executors, and creditors — often revealing a whole generation of family members. Probate was handled at the county level.

### Tax Lists / Assessment Records
Provincial and state tax lists exist for Pennsylvania in the 1770s–1780s and are an important substitute when vital records are absent. They place a person in a specific township in a specific year. The **Pennsylvania State Archives** holds many originals; some are microfilmed at FamilySearch.

### 1790 Federal Census
The first federal census (1790) covers the end of your target window. It lists heads of household with counts of free white males (by age bracket), free white females, and enslaved persons. It does not name everyone in the household, but it confirms presence in a county and township. Available on [FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org) and Ancestry.

### Revolutionary War Records (1775–1783)
The war falls squarely within your target period. **NARA pension files** (available on Fold3 and FamilySearch) can provide age, birthplace, names of spouse and children. However, note the Quaker pacifist dimension: many Friends refused military service and were **disowned by their meeting** as a result — those disownment records at the monthly meeting level may be more valuable for Quaker families than pension files.

### Newspapers
*Pennsylvania Gazette* and other colonial-era papers published death notices and occasionally marriage notices. **Newspapers.com** and the **Library of Congress Chronicling America** project are the best access points.

---

## Research Strategy for Quaker Families

1. **Identify the monthly meeting** — Find what monthly meeting served the township where your ancestor lived. The FamilySearch Wiki article *Pennsylvania Church Records* and the Hinshaw *Encyclopedia* both organize entries by meeting name.

2. **Search Hinshaw first** — Check the published abstract before browsing original volumes.

3. **Search [Church and Town Records, 1708–1985](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638) on FamilySearch** — This indexed collection is your fastest online path to meeting register entries.

4. **Browse the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes (volume 001188322)** — Browse-only, 145 images, covers 1750–1800. Useful for broader organizational context and identifying prominent families.

5. **Contact Friends Historical Library (Swarthmore) or Haverford** — For records of specific monthly meetings not yet digitized, these are the definitive physical repositories.

6. **Layer in land, probate, and tax records** — These will confirm county and township residence and fill gaps between meeting record entries.

7. **Watch for certificates of removal** — If an ancestor disappears from one county's records, a removal certificate may show exactly where they went next, making these documents powerful migration tracers.

---

## Coverage Gaps to Note

- **No statewide civil registration for this period** — church/meeting records carry the full weight of vital documentation.
- The `volume_search` returned only **one digitized Quaker volume** (Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes), and it is **browse-only with no name index**. The bulk of individual monthly meeting registers are held physically at Swarthmore and Haverford or on microfilm — not yet name-indexed online.
- The `external_links_search` returned only generic repository links (Ancestry census, NARA, FindAGrave, Newspapers.com, Fold3) — no Pennsylvania-Quaker-specific third-party databases were surfaced for this time window.
- Population data was unavailable for this period from the tool, but Wikipedia confirms Quaker concentration was densest in **Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware counties** in the late 1700s.

---

## Quick-Reference Repository Table

| Repository | What's There | How to Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** | Original PA monthly meeting registers, minutes, certificates of removal | In-person; some microfilm via FamilySearch |
| **Haverford College Quaker Collections** | Philadelphia-region meeting records | In-person |
| **FamilySearch** | Church & Town Records (indexed), Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes (browse-only) | Free at familysearch.org |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg** | Tax lists, land warrants, county records | In-person and some online |
| **NARA** | Revolutionary War pension files, 1790 census | Fold3, Ancestry, FamilySearch |
| **Hinshaw Encyclopedia** (published) | Abstracts from PA monthly meeting records | Libraries; some digitized copies online |

Quaker records for Pennsylvania in this period are genuinely among the richest in colonial American genealogy — the challenge is less about whether records exist and more about locating which monthly meeting your family belonged to and whether that meeting's register has been microfilmed. That identification step, using Hinshaw and the FamilySearch Wiki, is the most important first move.

---

# Pittsburgh, Allegheny County, Pennsylvania — Locality Research Guide
**Target Period: c. 1870–1900 | Focus: Steel Industry & Occupational Records**

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Pittsburgh (est. as city 1816) sits in **Allegheny County** (est. 1788), at the confluence of the Allegheny, Monongahela, and Ohio rivers. All county-level civil records — naturalizations, deeds, probate, marriages — were created and are still held at the Allegheny County level. **Pennsylvania did not require statewide vital registration until 1906**, so birth, marriage, and death records for 1870–1900 are found in city/county registers, church records, and FamilySearch's Pennsylvania-level collections — not in a central state repository.

The late 1800s brought massive immigration to Pittsburgh's iron and steel mills (Irish, German, Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian), making ethnic church records and naturalization files indispensable complements to secular civil sources.

---

## Record Classes — Access Levels & Repositories

### 🏭 Occupational & Industrial Records *(primary focus)*

| Record Type | What It Contains | Repository | Online Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Company Employment Records** | Hiring dates, wages, job grade, sometimes nationality & next-of-kin | Heinz History Center; Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area archives | **Physical only** — fragmentary survival; not on FamilySearch or Ancestry |
| **Union Membership Lists** | Name, local number, admission date, trade grade | Heinz History Center (Amalgamated Assoc. of Iron, Steel & Tin Workers records, founded 1876) | **Physical only** |
| **State Factory Inspector Reports** | Pennsylvania factory inspection began 1889; annual reports name mills, list accident victims and sometimes employees by trade | Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh | **Physical / microfilm** — some reports digitized via HathiTrust and Google Books |
| **City Directories** | Name, occupation, employer, street address — annual snapshots | Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh holds Pittsburgh directories from the 1810s; FamilySearch has some digitized volumes | **Partial — browse-only images** on FamilySearch; fuller run at Carnegie Library |
| **Federal Census Occupational Schedules** | 1870, 1880 census industry/manufacturing schedules list individual firms and workforces by county | NARA (originals); many microfilmed; some on Ancestry & FamilySearch | **Indexed + images** on Ancestry (U.S. Federal Census Collection); FamilySearch free |
| **Mortality Schedules (1870, 1880)** | Deaths in the year preceding the census; include occupation | FamilySearch; Ancestry | **Indexed + images** |

> **Gap note:** Pre-1900 private company employment records have very low survival rates nationally. The Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel archives hold the best-surviving Pittsburgh industrial documentation, but holdings are fragmentary. Contact both before assuming records don't exist.

---

### 📋 Census Records

The federal census is your most comprehensive tool for placing a worker in the mills:

- **1870, 1880, 1900** federal census (Allegheny County) — list name, age, birthplace, parents' birthplaces (1880+), occupation, employer (1880 and 1900 partial) — **indexed + images** on Ancestry and FamilySearch
- **1890 federal census** — almost entirely destroyed by fire; treat as lost for Allegheny County
- **Pennsylvania State Census** — Pennsylvania did not conduct its own decennial census in this period; no state-level gap-filler exists

---

### ⛪ Church Records *(critical for immigrant steel workers)*

The FamilySearch collection **"Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985"** (1,245,830 records; 607,220 images) is the main digitized gateway, but coverage by individual parish varies:

- **Catholic ethnic parishes** (Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian) were the primary religious home for most late-arriving mill workers; their registers recorded baptisms, marriages, and burials with far more family detail than civil records
- **Protestant denominations** (Presbyterian, Lutheran, Episcopal) served older English, German, and Scots-Irish populations
- Parish registers not yet on FamilySearch may be held at diocesan archives (Diocese of Pittsburgh) or still at the local parish

> Digitization level for church records: **partial indexed + images** for what is in the FamilySearch collection; a significant portion remains **browse-only** or **physical only** at the parish/diocesan level.

---

### 💍 Marriage Records

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania, County Marriages | 1885–1950 | 524,189 | 312,045 | Indexed + images — FamilySearch |
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages | 1677–1950 | 312,045 | 156,023 | Indexed + images — FamilySearch |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages | 1700–1900 | 198,765 | 0 | **Index only** — no images; physical originals elsewhere |
| Church registers (Catholic/Protestant) | Varies by parish | — | Partial | See Church Records above |

> Pre-1885, marriage records in Allegheny County are primarily found in church registers and the compiled/civil marriage indexes above. The compiled marriages index has no images — use it to identify a marriage, then seek the original at the county courthouse or church.

---

### ⚰️ Death Records

- **Pennsylvania Death Certificates begin in 1906** — *after* the target period. This is a significant gap.
- **Pre-1906 deaths** are documented through: city/county death registers (Pittsburgh began local registration in the 1870s, held at Allegheny County); church burial registers; cemetery records; newspaper obituaries; and the 1870/1880 mortality census schedules
- **Allegheny County death registers (1870s–1905)**: held at the Allegheny County Health Department and partially available through FamilySearch and the Heinz History Center
- **Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906–1968** (4,218,763 records + images on FamilySearch) — useful for deaths of aging steel workers just after the target window

---

### 📜 Naturalization Records *(essential for immigrant workers)*

Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in *any* court of record — federal, state, or local:

- **Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas** naturalization dockets — primary repository; held at Allegheny County courthouse; partially microfilmed by FamilySearch
- **U.S. District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania** — held at National Archives at Philadelphia (NARA Mid-Atlantic, Philadelphia, PA)
- FamilySearch has microfilmed some Pennsylvania naturalization records; search under "Pennsylvania, Naturalization Records" in the catalog
- The Heinz History Center also holds some naturalization-related indices

> **Tip:** Pre-1906 declarations of intent ("first papers") and petitions for naturalization often recorded country/town of origin, occupation, and witnesses — making them richer than they look.

---

### 🏛️ Probate Records

**Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** — 893,214 records; 446,607 images — indexed + images on FamilySearch. Wills and estate inventories sometimes list occupation, employer, and personal property (tools, equipment) that confirm a mill worker identity.

---

### 📰 Newspapers

Pittsburgh's newspapers — especially the *Pittsburgh Post-Gazette* (est. 1786) and *Pittsburgh Press* — covered mill accidents, strikes (notably Homestead 1892), deaths, and immigration extensively:

- **Newspapers.com** — major Pittsburgh papers digitized and keyword-searchable
- **GenealogyBank** — additional Pittsburgh newspaper holdings
- **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** — microfilm runs of local papers; some issues not yet digitized

---

### 🎖️ Military Records

Many steel-era workers were Civil War veterans; pension files are genealogically rich:

- **Civil War pension files** — NARA; searchable on **Fold3** and **Ancestry**
- **Spanish-American War (1898)** draft and service records — National Archives
- Pennsylvania adjutant general records — Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What They Hold | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Senator John Heinz History Center** (Pittsburgh) | Industrial/company records, immigration records, union records, city directories, photographs, labor history | In-person; some digital via Detre Library & Archives |
| **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** | Steel industry records, mill photographs, oral histories | In-person; contact for access |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** | City directories (1810s–), local history, Pittsburgh newspaper collection | In-person + some digital |
| **Allegheny County Courthouse** | Naturalizations, deeds, probate, marriage licenses | In-person; some microfilmed at FamilySearch |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg)** | Factory inspector reports, state-level records, PA National Guard | In-person + some digital |
| **NARA Mid-Atlantic (Philadelphia)** | Federal court naturalizations, federal census originals, federal agency records | In-person; many digitized |
| **Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** | Catholic parish registers for ethnic communities | Contact for access |
| **FamilySearch (free, online)** | Pennsylvania Church and Town Records, County Marriages, Probate Records, some Allegheny County materials | Online at familysearch.org |
| **Ancestry.com (subscription)** | U.S. Federal Census 1870–1900, city directories, newspapers, military records | Subscription (free at many public libraries) |

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Type | Digitization Level |
|---|---|
| Federal Census 1870, 1880, 1900 | ✅ Indexed + images (Ancestry, FamilySearch) |
| Pennsylvania County/Civil Marriages 1885–1900 | ✅ Indexed + images (FamilySearch) |
| Pennsylvania Compiled Marriages to 1900 | ⚠️ Index only — no images |
| Pennsylvania Church & Town Records | ⚠️ Partially indexed + images; gaps remain |
| Pennsylvania Probate Records | ✅ Indexed + images (FamilySearch) |
| City Directories | ⚠️ Browse-only images (FamilySearch); fuller run at Carnegie Library |
| Mortality Schedules 1870, 1880 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Pre-1906 Death Registers (Allegheny Co.) | ⚠️ Partial — some microfilmed; check Heinz History Center |
| State Factory Inspector Reports | ⚠️ Partial — some on HathiTrust/Google Books; physical at State Archives |
| Company Employment Records | 🔴 Physical only — fragmentary survival |
| Union Membership Records | 🔴 Physical only — Heinz History Center |
| Naturalization Records (county/federal courts) | ⚠️ Partially microfilmed; originals at courthouse and NARA |

---

## Research Strategy for Steel-Era Pittsburgh Families

1. **Start with the federal census** (1870 → 1880 → 1900) to establish residence, household members, birthplaces, and occupation across time.
2. **Search city directories** annually between censuses — they fill the decade gaps and often name the specific mill or employer.
3. **Seek naturalization records** at Allegheny County courthouse and NARA Philadelphia — for immigrant workers these are often the only link to a European hometown.
4. **Identify the ethnic parish** using the census birthplace and neighborhood, then contact the Diocese of Pittsburgh or search FamilySearch's Church & Town Records collection.
5. **Check the Heinz History Center** for surviving company and union records — contact the Detre Library & Archives before visiting.
6. **Mine newspapers** (Newspapers.com, GenealogyBank) for accident reports, strike coverage, death notices, and naturalization notices.
7. **Rivers of Steel** is a specialized resource for mill-specific context and some surviving company photographs and records — worth a targeted inquiry for workers at Carnegie, Jones & Laughlin, or other named mills.

# Locality Guide: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania — Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Formed:** Philadelphia County, 1682, from the original Penn land grant; co-terminus with Philadelphia City since 1854 (the Consolidation Act), but in 1793 the city and county were separate jurisdictions sharing the same seat
- **County seat:** Philadelphia (also the U.S. national capital, 1790–1800)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Pennsylvania (state from 1776; Commonwealth)
- **Population during period:** approximately 50,000 residents in the city proper in 1793, making it the largest city in the new nation
- **Economy:** Atlantic port trade, mercantile commerce, artisan manufacturing, printing and publishing; Philadelphia was the financial and governmental hub of the early republic
- **Ethnic/religious composition:** English Quakers (dominant founding group), German Lutherans and Reformed, Scots-Irish Presbyterians, Welsh, Dutch, Sephardic Jews, and a substantial free Black community; also Catholic Irish and German immigrants
- **Key historical event:** The yellow fever epidemic struck August–November 1793, killing approximately 5,000 people — roughly one-tenth of the city's population. An estimated 10,000–17,000 residents fled the city. The epidemic recurred in 1797, 1798, and 1799, making the late 1790s a period of extraordinary mortality in Philadelphia's record landscape. (*Source: Wikipedia, "Yellow fever epidemic of 1793"*)

---

## Boundary Notes

Philadelphia County boundaries were stable in 1793. The city of Philadelphia occupied only a small portion of the county at this time; the surrounding districts (Southwark, Northern Liberties, Kensington, Germantown) were separate municipal units within the county but used the same county courts and many of the same churches. **Records may be held at either the city or county level — check both.** The 1854 Consolidation Act merged all of these into a single City-County; pre-1854 records created by Southwark, Northern Liberties, or other districts are housed at the Philadelphia City Archives.

---

## Epidemic-Specific Records — Start Here

These sources are unique to the 1793 outbreak and are the first stop for anyone researching an ancestor who died, fled, or provided relief during the epidemic.

### 1. Mathew Carey's Published List of the Dead
Mathew Carey, a Philadelphia printer, published *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever Lately Prevalent in Philadelphia* (1793, multiple editions). Later editions include an appendix listing names of persons who died during the epidemic. This is the most accessible single name list for epidemic fatalities.
- **Access:** Digitized and freely available at the Internet Archive (archive.org) and HathiTrust. Also held in print at HSP and the Library Company of Philadelphia.
- **Digitization level: Indexed + images** (via HathiTrust full-text search)

### 2. Minutes of the Committee Appointed by the Citizens of Philadelphia
The citizens' relief committee, chaired by Mayor Matthew Clarkson, met daily during the epidemic and kept detailed minutes recording relief distributions, hospital admissions, burials arranged, and names of the dead and destitute. These are a primary genealogical source.
- **Where held:** Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP), Philadelphia
- **Access:** Physical manuscript; HSP reading room (appointment required). Some transcriptions have been published in historical journals.
- **Digitization level: Physical only**

### 3. Bush Hill Hospital Records
Bush Hill, a mansion on the edge of the city, was converted into an emergency hospital under the direction of Stephen Girard and Peter Helm. Admission and death records were kept, though survival is incomplete.
- **Where held:** Philadelphia City Archives; HSP may hold duplicates or transcriptions
- **Digitization level: Physical only** — inquire at both repositories before visiting

### 4. Free African Society and Black Community Records
The Free African Society, led by Absalom Jones and Richard Allen, organized much of the nursing and burial labor during the epidemic. Their minutes and reports name individuals and may document Black families largely invisible in other records.
- **Where held:** Historical Society of Pennsylvania; the African Episcopal Church of St. Thomas (Philadelphia)
- **Published source:** *A Narrative of the Proceedings of the Black People During the Late Awful Calamity in Philadelphia* (Jones & Allen, 1794) — digitized at HathiTrust
- **Digitization level: Published narrative indexed + images; manuscript records physical only**

---

## Vital Records (Civil Registration)

**Pennsylvania did not require statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths until 1906.** For 1793, there are no government vital records. Church registers are the only contemporaneous vital source.

---

## Church Records — Primary Source for 1793 Vital Events

Church burial registers are the backbone of 1793 research. During the epidemic, burial entries spike dramatically, and many registers note the cause of death as "yellow fever." The FamilySearch wiki article on *Philadelphia Genealogy* specifically identifies the following congregations as key sources:

### Key Philadelphia Congregations, c. 1793

| Congregation | Denomination | Record Types | Key Repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Christ Church** | Episcopal | Baptisms, burials, marriages from 1709 | HSP; FamilySearch microfilm |
| **St. Peter's Church** | Episcopal | Burials, baptisms from 1762 | HSP; FamilySearch microfilm |
| **Gloria Dei (Old Swedes')** | Lutheran (later Episcopal) | Burials from 1703 | HSP; digitized |
| **St. Mary's Catholic Church** | Roman Catholic | Burials, baptisms from 1760s | Philadelphia Archdiocesan Archives |
| **St. Michael's & Zion** | German Lutheran | Burials, baptisms from 1745 | HSP; Lutheran Archives Center |
| **First German Reformed** | German Reformed | Burials, baptisms | HSP |
| **First Presbyterian** | Presbyterian | Registers | Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia |
| **Mikveh Israel** | Jewish | Burial records | Mikveh Israel Congregation Archives |
| **Friends (Quaker) Monthly Meetings** | Society of Friends | Births, marriages, deaths (no sacramental register; recorded in meeting minutes) | Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College; HSP |
| **African Episcopal Church of St. Thomas** | Episcopal (Black congregation, founded 1792) | Registers | HSP |
| **Mother Bethel AME** | Methodist (Black congregation, founded 1793) | Early registers | Mother Bethel AME Church |

**FamilySearch Collection — Church and Town Records, 1708–1985**
FamilySearch holds a Pennsylvania statewide collection: *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* (Collection ID 1401638), containing 1,245,830 records with 607,220 images.
- This collection includes many Philadelphia church registers and covers the 1793 period
- **Digitization level: Indexed + images** — searchable by name at familysearch.org (free)
- **URL:** https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638

**Language note:** German Lutheran and German Reformed registers are typically in German. Latin phrases appear in Catholic registers.

**Physical repositories:** Many original registers remain with the congregations or at HSP. The Presbyterian Historical Society (425 Lombard St., Philadelphia) holds Presbyterian records. The Lutheran Archives Center at Philadelphia (Germantown) holds German Lutheran records. The Archdiocesan Archives holds Catholic records.

---

## Census Records

| Census | Year | Philadelphia Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| First U.S. Federal Census | 1790 | Philadelphia City and County enumerated; heads of household named only | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch, Ancestry, free online |
| Second U.S. Federal Census | 1800 | Full Philadelphia enumeration; heads of household named | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| Pennsylvania State Census | None | Pennsylvania conducted no state census in this period | N/A |

**Mortality schedules** did not begin until the 1850 census; they do not exist for the 1793 epidemic.

**Epidemic displacement note:** Thousands of Philadelphians fled to surrounding counties (Chester, Delaware, Montgomery, Bucks) and to New Jersey and other states during the epidemic. If an ancestor disappears from Philadelphia records in 1793, check the 1790 and 1800 censuses of neighboring counties for refugee settlement patterns.

---

## Probate and Orphans' Court Records

Given that ~5,000 people died in a matter of weeks, estate administration filings in late 1793 and 1794 are exceptionally dense and valuable. Wills, intestate administrations, inventories, and guardianships for minor children all appear here.

**FamilySearch Collection — Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** (Collection ID 1417683): 893,214 records, 446,607 images — covers Philadelphia County
- **Digitization level: Indexed + images** — https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683

**Physical repository:** Original Philadelphia County Orphans' Court records are at the **Philadelphia City Archives** (3101 Market St., Philadelphia). The Register of Wills for Philadelphia County also holds original wills and administration bonds.

**Research tip:** After a mass-death event like yellow fever, guardianship petitions for orphaned children are especially numerous. Searching guardianship dockets for 1793–1796 can identify children whose parents died in the epidemic.

---

## Land Records

- **System:** Metes and bounds (Pennsylvania is a state-land state; the Penn proprietors issued warrants and patents)
- **Types:** Warrants, surveys, patents, deeds, and mortgages
- **Where held:** Philadelphia County deed books at the **Philadelphia City Archives**; Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg) holds original warrant/patent records from the General Land Office of Pennsylvania
- **City Directories (substitute source):** Philadelphia had printed city directories beginning with Macpherson's *Philadelphia Directory* (1785), updated annually through the 1790s. These document residents' names, occupations, and addresses — invaluable for tracking who remained in the city vs. who fled and for identifying heads of household not captured in census years
  - **Access:** Digitized at HathiTrust and Ancestry; also on microfilm at HSP

---

## Marriage Records

**FamilySearch Collection — Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950** (Collection ID 1571957): 312,045 records, 156,023 images
- **Digitization level: Indexed + images** — https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957

**FamilySearch Collection — Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900** (Collection ID 1502909): 198,765 records, **no images**
- **Digitization level: Index only** — useful for leads; must verify against original register

For 1793, church marriage registers are more complete than civil records. See Church Records above.

---

## Military Records

The relevant conflict for 1793 Philadelphia is the Revolutionary War (1775–1783), already concluded; the Quasi-War with France (1798–1800) and Northwest Indian Wars (early 1790s) also involved Pennsylvanians. Some epidemic-period men served in Pennsylvania militia.

- **Revolutionary War pension files:** National Archives (NARA); fully digitized and indexed at Fold3.com (subscription) and partially free via Ancestry
- **Pennsylvania Militia records:** Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; also partially in the published *Pennsylvania Archives* series (multiple series, freely available at archive.org)
- **NARA Philadelphia Branch** (now at National Archives at Philadelphia, 900 Market St.): holds federal military and civilian records for this region

---

## Cemetery Records

Philadelphia's major burial grounds in 1793:

| Cemetery | Affiliation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Christ Church Burial Ground | Episcopal | Benjamin Franklin buried here; epidemic burials documented |
| Gloria Dei Churchyard | Swedish/Episcopal | Active burial ground 1700s onward |
| Arch Street Friends Meeting Burial Ground | Quaker | Large Quaker cemetery; records at HSP |
| St. Mary's Catholic Burial Ground | Catholic | Earliest Catholic cemetery in Philadelphia |
| Mikveh Israel Cemetery | Jewish | Oldest Jewish burial ground in Philadelphia |
| African American cemeteries | Various | The Free African Society arranged emergency burials; some in Potter's Field |

**Potter's Field / Public Burial Ground:** During the epidemic, many bodies — particularly the poor and anonymous dead — were buried in the public Potter's Field. These burials are often recorded in the Committee minutes rather than church registers.

**Online coverage:**
- **FindAGrave.com** (free): covers major Philadelphia cemeteries with volunteer-transcribed entries; coverage is uneven for 1793
- **BillionGraves.com** (free): supplemental coverage; less complete for pre-1800 markers
- **Digitization level for sexton registers:** Physical only at church archives and HSP; FindAGrave and BillionGraves are derivative volunteer transcriptions, not official records

---

## Newspaper Records

Philadelphia had an active press in 1793. Newspapers published death notices, lists of the sick, notices of people who had fled, and reports of the Committee during the epidemic. These are extraordinary sources for named individuals.

| Paper | Years | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| *Federal Gazette* | 1788–1798 | Published daily epidemic death lists in August–November 1793 |
| *General Advertiser* (later *Aurora*) | 1790–1835 | Benjamin Franklin Bache, editor; epidemic coverage |
| *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser* | 1784–1800 | Daily notices, deaths, legal notices |
| *Pennsylvania Gazette* | 1728–1800 | Long-established; estate notices, legal ads |

- **Genealogical Newspapers.com** (subscription): covers some Philadelphia papers from this era
- **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, free): https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov — covers some Pennsylvania papers; check for Philadelphia titles and 1793 dates
- **HSP newspaper collection:** HSP holds microfilm of most Philadelphia newspapers from this period
- **Digitization level:** Partial digital access via Chronicling America (free) and Newspapers.com (subscription); HSP holds microfilm for gaps

---

## Immigration and Naturalization Records

Philadelphia was the primary East Coast port of entry. Naturalization before 1906 could be filed in any court of record (federal, state, or local).

- **Passenger lists (Port of Philadelphia):** NARA holds records; digitized on Ancestry.com (*Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, Passenger and Crew Lists, 1800–1945*) — pre-1800 lists are sparse
- **Naturalization records:** Philadelphia Common Pleas Court naturalizations are at the Philadelphia City Archives; also partially on FamilySearch and Ancestry
- **Pennsylvania Archives, Series 2, Vol. 2:** Published records including some naturalization and oath-of-allegiance records from the founding era — freely available at archive.org
- **Digitization level:** Post-1800 passenger lists indexed + images on Ancestry; pre-1800 and naturalizations are largely browse-only or physical only

---

## Tax Records

Pennsylvania assessed a general property tax on residents. Tax assessment lists survive for Philadelphia and are valuable substitutes for the years between the 1790 and 1800 censuses — they name adult male property holders and tenants.

- **Where held:** Philadelphia City Archives; Pennsylvania State Archives; HSP (transcripts)
- **Published:** Some Philadelphia tax records appear in the *Pennsylvania Archives* series
- **1793 tax lists** would document who was present and taxable before the epidemic; comparing to 1794 lists can reveal disappearances (flight or death)
- **Digitization level:** Partially indexed on Ancestry (*Pennsylvania, Tax Records, 1782–1850*); originals are physical at the repositories above

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Relevant Collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Pennsylvania Church & Town Records 1708–1985 (1.2M records); Pennsylvania Probate Records 1683–1994 (893K records); Pennsylvania Civil Marriages 1677–1950; Pennsylvania Compiled Marriages 1700–1900; 1790 & 1800 federal censuses | **Free** |
| **Ancestry.com** | Pennsylvania tax records; Philadelphia passenger lists; Philadelphia city directories; some church records; federal censuses | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Revolutionary War pension files and military records | Subscription |
| **HathiTrust / Internet Archive** | Mathew Carey's epidemic account; *Pennsylvania Archives* series; Philadelphia city directories 1785–1800s; historical monographs | **Free** |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov) | Some Philadelphia newspapers from the 1790s | **Free** |
| **Newspapers.com** | Philadelphia newspapers (broader coverage) | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Cemetery transcriptions for major Philadelphia burial grounds | **Free** |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Key Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Philadelphia City Archives** (3101 Market St.) | City and county government records; Orphans' Court; pre-1854 district records; tax lists; Board of Health records | In-person; some records by appointment |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** (1300 Locust St.) | Church register transcripts; Committee minutes; city directories; newspapers on microfilm; manuscript collections; Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania papers | In-person reading room; appointment recommended |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society** (425 Lombard St.) | Presbyterian church registers for Philadelphia and PA | In-person |
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** | Quaker Monthly Meeting records (births, marriages, deaths, disownments) | In-person |
| **Lutheran Archives Center at Philadelphia** (7301 Germantown Ave.) | German Lutheran church records; St. Michael's & Zion registers | In-person |
| **Archdiocesan Archives, Archdiocese of Philadelphia** | Catholic parish registers; St. Mary's burial records | By appointment |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Provincial records; warrant/patent records; PA Archives series | In-person; some records online |
| **National Archives at Philadelphia** (900 Market St.) | Federal records; post-1800 passenger lists; federal court naturalizations | In-person |
| **Library Company of Philadelphia** (1314 Locust St.) | Rare books, pamphlets, early newspapers; Mathew Carey papers | In-person; some digital |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following exist but are **not** available in any searchable online database:

- **Committee of Citizens minutes and reports** (1793 epidemic) — manuscript at HSP
- **Bush Hill hospital admission/death records** — manuscript at Philadelphia City Archives/HSP; survival status uncertain
- **Most original Philadelphia church registers** — originals held at congregations or HSP; FamilySearch microfilm/images exist for some but are not comprehensive
- **Philadelphia County Orphans' Court dockets, 1793–1796** — originals at Philadelphia City Archives
- **Philadelphia tax assessment lists pre-1800** — originals at Philadelphia City Archives and Pennsylvania State Archives
- **Free African Society minutes** — manuscript at HSP

---

## Known Record Gaps

- **No Pennsylvania civil vital registration before 1906** — church records are the only contemporaneous substitute
- **No mortality schedules before 1850** — the census mortality schedule series does not cover 1793
- **1790 census name coverage:** Only heads of household are named; other household members appear as tick marks by age/sex category
- **Poor and transient dead:** Many epidemic victims buried in Potter's Field were unaffiliated with churches and left no burial register entry; the Committee minutes are the closest substitute
- **Potter's Field records:** Fragmentary at best; some names appear in the Committee minutes

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with church burial registers and the Carey list in parallel.** The spike in burial entries August–November 1793 makes yellow fever victims easy to spot in registers. Mathew Carey's appendix (free at HathiTrust) provides a quick name-search starting point.

2. **Search the 1793–1795 probate filings.** The massive death toll means an unusual concentration of estate administrations in this narrow window. FamilySearch's Pennsylvania Probate Records collection (indexed + images) is the fastest path.

3. **Compare 1790 and 1800 census entries.** Households that shrank dramatically between censuses may reflect epidemic deaths. Conversely, households that moved to neighboring counties in 1800 may be epidemic refugees.

4. **Philadelphia city directories (1785–1800s)** are a census substitute between the 1790 and 1800 federal enumerations. Annual directories let you track a family year by year; a name disappearing between the 1793 and 1794 editions is a strong indicator of death or flight. Freely digitized at HathiTrust.

5. **Check surrounding counties for refugees.** Chester, Delaware, Montgomery, and Bucks counties received thousands of Philadelphia refugees in late 1793. Church records and tax lists in those counties may document your ancestor during and after the epidemic.

6. **Quaker records are unusually complete.** Friends Monthly Meeting records document births, marriages, deaths, and removals (transfers between meetings) with great detail. If your ancestor was Quaker, the Friends Historical Library at Swarthmore is an essential stop.

7. **The Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** has published extensive indexes and transcripts of Philadelphia church records, many predating digitization. HSP holds the GSP's compiled manuscript indexes, which may name individuals not yet in any online database.

8. **Board of Health records:** The Philadelphia Board of Health was formally constituted partly in response to the 1793 epidemic. Its early records, held at the Philadelphia City Archives, may contain lists of epidemic dead and relief recipients.

9. **FamilySearch's Pennsylvania Church and Town Records** collection (Collection 1401638) is indexed and free — run a name search there first before visiting repositories in person.

10. **For Black ancestors:** The Free African Society and Mother Bethel AME Church records are essential. The published *Narrative* by Jones and Allen (1794, free at HathiTrust) names some individuals. The African Episcopal Church of St. Thomas registers begin in 1792.
