# Locality Guide: Chicago / Cook County, Illinois — Great Chicago Fire Research (c. 1850–1900)

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Formed:** Cook County was established in 1831 from unorganized territory; Chicago was incorporated as a city in 1837.
- **County seat / administrative center:** Chicago
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Illinois (admitted to statehood 1818)
- **Population during period:** Population data was not returned by the FamilySearch tools, but Chicago grew from roughly 30,000 (1850) to nearly 300,000 by 1870, making it one of the fastest-growing cities in the world — and a city whose records infrastructure had not kept pace with that growth when the fire struck.
- **Economy:** Commercial hub — rail terminus, meatpacking, lumber, grain trade, manufacturing. Occupational records from these industries are an important supplement to destroyed civil records.
- **Ethnic composition:** Heavy concentrations of Irish, German, Scandinavian, and Bohemian immigrants in 1871; large African-American community. Ethnic enclaves maintained their own churches and foreign-language newspapers — both critical sources when civil records were destroyed.
- **Religious denominations:** Catholic (Irish and German parishes), Lutheran (German and Scandinavian), Methodist, Presbyterian, Episcopal, and Jewish congregations were all present by 1871.
- **Key historical event — the fire:** On October 8–10, 1871, the Great Chicago Fire burned approximately 3.3 square miles, killed roughly 300 people, and left more than 100,000 residents homeless. It destroyed the Cook County Courthouse, which held deeds, birth and death registers, court records, and tax rolls — creating a significant and permanent gap in Chicago's genealogical documentation. **(Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire")**

---

## Boundary Changes

Cook County's boundaries were stable throughout the target period (1850–1900) and remain essentially unchanged today. No boundary-change complications affect which records to seek or where to find them. All Cook County records for this period were created by and for the same jurisdictional unit.

---

## The Critical Divide: Before vs. After October 10, 1871

The fire is the single most important fact shaping every research strategy for this place. **Treat 1871 as a hard line:**

- **Pre-fire (before Oct. 10, 1871):** Most county-level civil records are **destroyed**. Research pivots to federal records (census, military), surviving church registers, and reconstructed property records.
- **Post-fire (from Oct. 10, 1871 onward):** Cook County began re-creating its civil record infrastructure almost immediately. Four substantial FamilySearch collections cover this window.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

Illinois did not require **statewide** vital records registration until 1916. Cook County/Chicago, however, began local registration earlier:

| Record Type | Dates | Collection | Record Count | Digitization Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Birth Certificates | 1871–1940 | *Illinois, Cook County, Birth Certificates, 1871–1940* (FS #2024189) | 1,450,000 | **Indexed + images** |
| Death Records | 1871–1947 | *Illinois, Cook County, Death Records, 1871–1947* (FS #1913456) | 980,000 | **Indexed + images** |
| Marriage Records | 1871–1920 | *Illinois, Cook County, Marriage Records, 1871–1920* (FS #1756321) | 620,000 | **Indexed + images** |

> ⚠️ **Gap:** Civil birth, death, and marriage records **before October 1871 are destroyed**. No Cook County civil vital records from that period survive. Use church records and the 1870 census as primary substitutes.

**Access:** All three collections are on FamilySearch (free). The Cook County Clerk's office also holds originals for vital records from 1871 onward and can issue certified copies.

---

### Church Records

Church registers are the **single most important substitute** for destroyed pre-fire civil vital records. Some parish registers survived the fire; others were re-established in the weeks after and recorded what members could reconstruct from memory. The FamilySearch Wiki notes that church records from parishes that "survived or were re-established" are a primary strategy for the pre-1871 period.

| Collection | Dates | Record Count | Digitization Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925* (FS #1803978) | 1833–1925 | 285,000 | **Indexed + images** |

- **Catholic records** — The Archdiocese of Chicago's **Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** holds Catholic parish registers. Many Irish and German parishes were established in the 1840s–1860s; some registers predate the fire. Contact: Archdiocese of Chicago Archives, 835 N. Rush St., Chicago.
- **Protestant and other denominations** — Records are scattered at individual congregations or in the **Newberry Library's** manuscript holdings. The **Chicago History Museum** also holds some congregational records.
- **Ethnic-language churches** — German Lutheran, Scandinavian Lutheran, and Bohemian parishes often recorded in their heritage language. Flag for translation needs.

**Research tip:** When using church records for pre-fire events, look for *both* the original register (if surviving) *and* any reconstruction entries made after the fire, which a diligent pastor may have created from memory or from parishioner affidavits.

---

### Census Records

Federal census records are **the backbone of pre-fire research** because the originals were held in Washington, D.C. — not at the Cook County Courthouse — and survived intact.

| Census Year | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **1850** | Survived | Indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| **1860** | Survived | Indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| **1870** | Survived | **Critical:** This is the last pre-fire snapshot of Chicago families. Indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| **1880** | Survived | First post-fire enumeration; shows rebuilt households |
| **1890** | Largely destroyed (1921 fire at NARA) | Only fragments survive nationally |
| **1900** | Survived | Indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry |

> The **1870 federal census** is your most important pre-fire document. It names every household member, gives ages, birthplaces, and occupations — use it to establish who was in Chicago before the disaster.

Illinois also conducted a **state census in 1865** (covering heads of household only). Check the Illinois State Archives for surviving schedules.

**Mortality schedules** (1850, 1860, 1870) record deaths in the preceding year and can document family members who died before the fire.

**Access:** Ancestry.com (subscription) via the *U.S. Federal Census Collection* (curated external link returned for this place). FamilySearch also holds census images free of charge.

---

### Land and Property Records

Land records are the most elaborately documented substitute category for the fire period, because Illinois passed specific legislation to address their loss.

**The Burnt Record Act of 1872** required property owners to re-register their deeds with the Cook County Recorder. The Wikipedia article on the Great Chicago Fire notes that insurance company records — especially the **Chicago Title and Trust Company collection** — "became vital substitute sources for pre-fire property ownership."

FamilySearch's `volume_search` returned two directly relevant digitized volumes:

| Volume | Image Group | Dates | Images | Indexing | Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-fire County Court Records (reconstructed copies) | 009982110 | 1865–1871 | 38 | **0% indexed, not full-text** | **Browse-only** — must be examined image by image |
| Post-fire Property Re-recordings | 009982111 | 1871–1880 | 112 | **41% indexed** | Partially searchable by name; remainder browse-only |

> ⚠️ The 1865–1871 reconstructed court records volume (38 images) is completely unindexed and not full-text searchable — you must browse every image manually. It is a small but potentially high-value set for tracing individuals immediately before the fire.

**Chicago Title and Trust Company records** (held at the **Chicago History Museum**) are physical-only; contact the museum's research services for access.

**Federal land patents** for Cook County (earlier land grants from the federal government) are available free through the Bureau of Land Management's General Land Office website (glorecords.blm.gov).

---

### Probate and Court Records

The fire destroyed probate files and most court records held at the Cook County Courthouse. No pre-fire probate or civil court records survive in significant quantity.

- **Post-fire probate records** (from 1871 onward): Held at the **Cook County Circuit Court Archives**. FamilySearch has microfilmed many of these; check the catalog under Cook County, Illinois.
- **Naturalizations** (pre-1906): Illinois naturalizations could be filed in any court of record. Pre-fire naturalizations are largely lost; post-fire naturalizations are available at the **Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at Northeastern Illinois University (NEIU)**. IRAD is the primary repository for Cook County court and government records after 1871.
- **Reconstructed court records (1865–1871):** The browse-only volume (009982110, 38 images) on FamilySearch represents reconstructed copies of some pre-fire county court records. Browse-only access only.

---

### Military Records

Military records were held federally — not at the Cook County Courthouse — so the fire had **no effect on them**.

- **Civil War service records and pension files:** At NARA (Washington, D.C. and Kansas City). Searchable on Fold3 (subscription) — returned as a curated external resource for this place.
- **Illinois Civil War rosters:** The Illinois State Archives holds state-level militia and volunteer rosters. FamilySearch has microfilmed portions.
- **Draft registrations (Civil War):** The 1863 draft registration (Consolidated List) covers Cook County men. Available on Ancestry and FamilySearch.
- **Veterans' schedules (1890 census):** Although the main 1890 census is largely destroyed, Illinois has partial Veterans' Schedules — check NARA and FamilySearch.

---

### Cemetery Records

Cemeteries were mostly outside the fire zone and their records survived.

- **Graceland Cemetery** (est. 1860) and **Rosehill Cemetery** (est. 1859) were major Chicago non-sectarian cemeteries active in 1871; both maintain burial records.
- **Catholic cemeteries** — Records held by individual cemeteries and the Archdiocese.
- **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) — returned as a curated external resource for Cook County. Covers many Chicago area cemeteries with headstone transcriptions and photographs.
- Sexton records for some cemeteries are available at the **Chicago History Museum** or **Newberry Library**.

---

### Newspaper Records

Chicago newspapers are an underused source for the fire period. Several major papers (including the *Chicago Tribune*) survived the fire, resumed publication within days, and printed extensive coverage — including lists of fire victims, displaced residents, and relief recipients. Obituaries, legal notices, and community news also appear in post-fire papers.

- **Newspapers.com** (subscription) — returned as a curated external resource; holds digitized Chicago papers including the *Chicago Tribune* from this period.
- **Newberry Library** holds microfilm of many Chicago-area newspapers including ethnic-language papers (German, Scandinavian, Czech).
- **Chicago History Museum** holds additional newspaper runs.

> **Tip:** Search newspaper archives for fire victim lists, relief rolls, and insurance claim notices published in October–December 1871. These can locate families who were in Chicago at the moment of the disaster.

---

### Immigration and Naturalization Records

Chicago was a major immigrant destination by 1871. Most immigrants arrived via East Coast ports (New York, Baltimore, New Orleans) or Great Lakes ports.

- **Passenger arrival lists:** At NARA (series M237, M364 for New York; other ports separately). Indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch. Examine full manifests — children including infants are listed alongside parents.
- **Naturalization records (pre-1906):** Filed in various courts; largely destroyed at the county level by the fire. Post-fire naturalizations at IRAD/NEIU (see Court Records above). Federal naturalizations after September 27, 1906 are at USCIS/NARA.
- **Ethnic mutual aid society records:** German, Irish, Scandinavian, and Bohemian communities maintained mutual aid societies whose membership and death benefit records sometimes survive at the **Newberry Library** or **Chicago History Museum**.

---

### Tax Records

Pre-fire tax rolls were destroyed with the courthouse. However:

- **Post-1871 tax records** are held by the Cook County Assessor/Treasurer; access through IRAD at NEIU.
- The **Burnt Record Act re-registrations** (property re-recordings, 1872 onward) function partly as tax-record substitutes for establishing land ownership.
- **Federal direct tax lists** (1862–1866) are a pre-fire substitute held at NARA and partially available on Ancestry.

---

### Other Record Types of Special Value for Fire Research

**Relief records:** After the fire, the Chicago Relief and Aid Society distributed relief to tens of thousands of displaced residents and kept detailed registers. These records are held at the **Chicago History Museum** and are an extraordinary source — they may name every member of a displaced family, their pre-fire address, their occupation, and what assistance they received. *This is a must-search for any family known to have been in Chicago in October 1871.*

**Insurance records:** The **Chicago Title and Trust Company collection** at the Chicago History Museum documents pre-fire property ownership reconstructed from insurance surveys and plat maps — physical access only.

**City directories:** Chicago city directories from the 1840s onward survived the fire and are held at the **Newberry Library**. The FamilySearch Wiki notes these are a key pre-fire resource. They list heads of household by name, address, and occupation annually — useful for tracking families year-by-year in the absence of census records.

**Vital records abstracts created by the Illinois DAR and WPA:** In the 1930s–40s, Daughters of the American Revolution and Works Progress Administration workers transcribed and abstracted surviving church and civil records in Illinois. Some Cook County transcriptions are at the **Illinois State Archives** and the **Newberry Library**.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Collections for This Place | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Church Records 1833–1925 (285,000); Birth Certificates 1871–1940 (1,450,000); Death Records 1871–1947 (980,000); Marriage Records 1871–1920 (620,000); 2 digitized volumes including reconstructed court records and post-fire property re-recordings | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census collection (all surviving years); passenger lists; Civil War draft registrations; city directories (some) | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Civil War service records and pension files for Illinois soldiers | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery transcriptions for Chicago-area cemeteries | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Digitized Chicago newspapers including the *Chicago Tribune* | Subscription |
| **NARA / Archives.gov** | Federal census, military, passenger, and naturalization records | Free (some via partners) |
| **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents for Cook County | Free |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Key Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Chicago History Museum** | Chicago Title and Trust insurance records; Chicago Relief and Aid Society registers (fire refugees); personal papers; photographs; sexton records | In-person research; contact for appointments |
| **Newberry Library** (Chicago) | City directories 1840s+; ethnic-language newspapers; manuscript collections; maps; DAR/WPA transcriptions | In-person; some digitized finding aids online |
| **Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at Northeastern Illinois University** | Cook County post-1871 court records; probate files; naturalizations; government records | In-person; contact NEIU for access |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago — Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) from pre-fire through modern period | Contact in writing; some records restricted |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Vital records from 1871 onward; certified copies | In-person or mail request |
| **Cook County Circuit Court Archives** | Post-fire probate and court records | In-person |
| **Illinois State Archives** (Springfield) | State census 1865; Illinois Civil War rosters; statewide vital records from 1916; some county records transferred from IRAD | In-person and some online finding aids |

---

## Records NOT Online

These records exist but are **not available in any online database** and require in-person or mail contact:

- **Chicago Relief and Aid Society registers** (fire refugee lists, 1871–1872) — Chicago History Museum, physical only
- **Chicago Title and Trust Company insurance/property records** — Chicago History Museum, physical only
- **Pre-fire church registers that survived** — Many remain at individual parishes or at the Bernardin Archives; not all are microfilmed or digitized
- **Post-fire Cook County probate files in full** — IRAD/NEIU; FamilySearch holds microfilm of some but coverage is incomplete
- **Ethnic mutual aid society membership and death records** — Newberry Library and Chicago History Museum; not indexed online
- **Sexton records for Graceland and Rosehill cemeteries** — held by the cemeteries; not fully online
- **City directories pre-1871** — Newberry Library holds originals; some have been digitized but not fully indexed

---

## Known Record Losses

| What Was Lost | When | Where Destroyed | Substitutes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deeds and land title records | Oct. 8–10, 1871 | Cook County Courthouse | Burnt Record Act re-recordings (1872+); Chicago Title and Trust records (CHM) |
| Civil birth and death registers | Oct. 8–10, 1871 | Cook County Courthouse | Church baptism and burial registers; 1870 federal census |
| Civil marriage records | Oct. 8–10, 1871 | Cook County Courthouse | Church marriage registers |
| Tax rolls | Oct. 8–10, 1871 | Cook County Courthouse | Federal direct tax lists 1862–1866 (NARA/Ancestry); post-1871 county tax records |
| Probate files and court records | Oct. 8–10, 1871 | Cook County Courthouse | 38-image reconstructed volume on FamilySearch (browse-only); post-fire probate at IRAD |
| 1890 federal census | 1921 (NARA fire) | NARA, Washington, D.C. | 1890 Veterans' Schedules (partial); 1889 Chicago city directory; Illinois state records |

> **Note on the 1870 census:** The Wikipedia article specifically confirms the 1870 Chicago census schedules *survived* because originals were held in Washington. This makes the 1870 census unusually important for fire-period research — treat it as your anchor document for pre-fire household composition.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the 1870 census** to establish the family in Chicago before the fire, then pivot to post-fire civil records (birth/death/marriage collections on FamilySearch) to trace the same family forward. The gap is October 1871 itself.

2. **Search the Chicago Relief and Aid Society registers at the Chicago History Museum.** For families displaced by the fire, this may be the only contemporaneous record of their address, occupation, and family composition in 1871.

3. **Church records bridge the gap.** The FamilySearch collection *Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925* (285,000 records, indexed) covers both pre- and post-fire events and is free to search. Start here for baptisms, marriages, and burials that civil registers cannot provide.

4. **Don't overlook the ethnic press.** German, Scandinavian, Irish, and Bohemian newspapers published lists of fire dead, missing-person notices, and community relief information in October–November 1871. The Newberry Library holds microfilm of many of these.

5. **Browse the 38-image reconstructed court records volume.** FamilySearch volume 009982110 covers 1865–1871 and is 0% indexed — it must be examined image by image — but it's a small set (38 images) and could contain naturalization, probate, or other court records for your family.

6. **For property ownership**, work the Burnt Record Act re-recordings (FamilySearch volume 009982111, 41% indexed, 1871–1880) alongside the Chicago Title and Trust records at the Chicago History Museum. These two sources together are the main substitute for the destroyed deed books.

7. **Naming variation alert:** Many immigrant families anglicized names after arrival, or clerks recorded names phonetically. Search Chicago records under both the heritage-language form and likely anglicized variants.

8. **FamilySearch Wiki page** "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy" (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois_Genealogy) is the best single starting reference for this jurisdiction; the sections on Record Loss and Repositories are directly applicable to fire-era research.

I'll survey Louisiana's French colonial period records. Let me start by establishing the jurisdictional context.Now I'll run the full parallel survey batch.Now launching the full parallel survey batch:Now let me read the wiki article and the two collections that overlap with the French colonial period.Here is the locality guide for French colonial Louisiana, grounded in what the tools returned.

---

# 🗺️ Locality Guide — French Colonial Louisiana (c. 1699–1769)

## Jurisdictional Context

**Louisiana (La Louisiane)** was an administrative district of New France, claimed for France in 1682 by La Salle. New Orleans was founded in 1718. France formally ceded the colony west of the Mississippi to Spain via the **Treaty of Fontainebleau (1762)**, with Spain taking effective administrative control in **1769**. The eastern strip went to Britain. For genealogical purposes, the core French colonial era runs **1699–1769**.

**Critical implications for research:**
- **No civil registration existed.** The Catholic Church was the *only* legally recognized church; all vital records are church records. The Code Noir (*Black Code*) legally required Catholic baptism of enslaved persons, making parish registers the primary source for enslaved ancestors as well.
- **No U.S. counties yet.** Records are organized by ecclesiastical parish and colonial administrative districts, not the civil parishes formed later under American rule.
- **Dual repository problem.** Original administrative records are split between Louisiana repositories and archives in **France** — you must search both.

---

## Record Types, Repositories & Access

### 1. 📖 Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
**The single most important source for this period.**

- **What they contain:** Baptisms, marriages, and burials for free settlers, free people of color, and enslaved individuals. Marriage acts often name parents, godparents, and witnesses — a treasure trove of kinship data.
- **Earliest known registers:** St. Louis Cathedral, New Orleans — registers survive from **1720** onward (FamilySearch wiki).
- **FamilySearch collection:** **"Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925"** (Collection ID 1612905) — reported 987,654 records and 493,827 images. This collection's date range begins in 1718 and directly overlaps the French colonial period. ⚠️ *Indexing/searchability percentages could not be confirmed (access to detailed collection data was restricted during this session) — browse the collection directly at [familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905) to assess whether your parish is name-indexed or browse-only.*
- **Physical repository:** **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint building, New Orleans) holds original and microfilm registers.
- **Non-English alert:** Registers are in **French**; researchers must read colonial French script.

---

### 2. ⚖️ Superior Council (Conseil Supérieur) Records
**The French colonial government's court and administrative archive.**

- **What they contain:** Land concessions (grants), court proceedings, civil contracts, estate inventories, and legal disputes. Functionally equivalent to probate and court records combined.
- **Repository:** **Louisiana Historical Center** (New Orleans) — partially digitized (FamilySearch wiki). Originals and transcripts also exist at the **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)** in Aix-en-Provence, France.
- **FamilySearch collection:** **"Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984"** (Collection ID 2078341) — reported 456,789 records and 228,394 images. Date range begins 1756, which overlaps the final decade of French rule. ⚠️ *Same caveat as above — confirm indexing level by browsing the collection at [familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341).*
- **Non-English alert:** Documents are in **French**.

---

### 3. 📜 Notarial Records
**Among the richest sources for colonial family and property history.**

- **What they contain:** Marriage contracts (stipulating dowries and property arrangements), wills, bills of sale (including sales of enslaved persons), apprenticeship contracts, and commercial agreements. The **New Orleans Notarial Archives** is the oldest continuously operating notarial office in North America, with holdings from the **1730s onward**.
- **Repository:** **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** — provides public in-person access.
- **Online access:** Partial digitization; check the New Orleans Notarial Archives website for current online holdings.
- **Non-English alert:** Documents are in **French** (and later Spanish).

---

### 4. 🌾 Land Records & Concessions
**Essential for locating families geographically and tracking property.**

- **French concessions** (land grants) are documented in:
  - **American State Papers: Public Lands** series — the U.S. government adjudicated old French and Spanish grants after the Louisiana Purchase; these proceedings name original grantees and heirs.
  - **Louisiana State Land Office** — holds records of confirmed land grants.
  - The **Louisiana Historical Center** and **ANOM** hold original French concession records.
- **No BLM General Land Office records** apply to this period (the GLO system is post-1803 U.S. federal land).

---

### 5. 🇫🇷 Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM), France
**The mother archive for French colonial administration worldwide.**

- **Location:** Aix-en-Provence, France
- **What they hold:** Administrative correspondence between colonial governors and Paris, **colonial census rolls** (*recensements*), military rosters, passenger and emigrant lists, and some notarial records.
- **Key census rolls:** French colonial censuses of Louisiana (1699, 1708, 1721, 1724, 1726, 1731, 1752) are among the earliest population lists for the region. These are genealogically critical substitutes for any "census" record in this era.
- **Online access:** ANOM has a digitization portal at **archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr** and **mnesys-portail.archives-nationales.culture.gouv.fr**. Some Louisiana series have been partially digitized; physical visits or document orders may be needed for others.
- **Non-English alert:** All records are in **French**.

---

### 6. ⛪ Military Records
- French colonial Louisiana had regular troops (*troupes de la marine*) and colonial militia. **ANOM** holds muster rolls and officer records. Some transcripts appear in Louisiana historical society publications.
- The **Conseil Supérieur** records also document militia service.

---

### 7. 📰 Emigrant & Passenger Lists
- France sent organized colonist expeditions to Louisiana in the 1710s–1720s (including the John Law/Company of the Indies scheme). Passenger lists and emigrant contracts for these voyages are at **ANOM** and have been partly published in secondary works (e.g., *Emigrants to Louisiana, 1718–1724* by Winston De Ville).

---

## Collections Found on FamilySearch (Filtered to 1700–1769 overlap)

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | Overlaps Colonial Period |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925 | 1718–1925 | ~988,000 | ~494,000 | ✅ Yes — from 1718 |
| Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984 | 1756–1984 | ~457,000 | ~228,000 | ✅ Yes — from 1756 |
| Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957 | 1837–1957 | — | — | ❌ Post-period |
| Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964 | 1819–1964 | — | — | ❌ Post-period |

FamilySearch returned **4 total collections** for Louisiana; only 2 overlap the French colonial window. This is a coverage gap — the colonial period is thinly indexed on FamilySearch compared to later eras.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | Location | What's There |
|---|---|---|
| Louisiana Historical Center | New Orleans, LA (Old U.S. Mint) | Superior Council records, church registers, colonial docs |
| New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center | New Orleans, LA | Notarial acts from 1730s onward |
| Louisiana State Archives | Baton Rouge, LA | Land records, later colonial-era documents |
| Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM) | Aix-en-Provence, France | Admin correspondence, census rolls, military, emigrant lists |
| FamilySearch | Online | Church records (1718+), probate (1756+) |

---

## Important Gaps & Cautions

- **No civil vital registration** in this period — church records are the only vital source.
- **External link search returned only generic resources** (NARA, Ancestry census, Find A Grave) for this time window — none are Louisiana colonial-specific. The colonial period is largely absent from mass-market genealogy platforms.
- **Language barrier is real:** Virtually every record — parish registers, notarial acts, council proceedings, census rolls — is in **colonial French**. Paleography skills or a specialist translator are strongly recommended.
- **Record losses:** Some early registers were lost to fire, flood, and the disruptions of successive colonial governments. Notarial and court records often survive better than parish registers for the earliest decades (pre-1720).
- **Enslaved ancestors:** The Code Noir mandated Catholic baptism, so enslaved individuals frequently appear in parish registers by first name and enslaver's name. The Conseil Supérieur records also document sales and estates involving enslaved people. ANOM holds some plantation-level documentation.

---

## Recommended Starting Strategy

1. **Start with the FamilySearch Church Records collection (1718–1925)** — browse for your family's parish by location/time period.
2. **Identify the specific parish** (e.g., St. Louis Cathedral for New Orleans, St. Charles Borromeo for the German Coast) and contact the **Louisiana Historical Center** for that parish's microfilm or original register.
3. **Search the Conseil Supérieur records** at the Louisiana Historical Center for estate inventories and legal proceedings naming your ancestors.
4. **Search the New Orleans Notarial Archives** for marriage contracts and wills from the 1730s onward.
5. **Order from or visit ANOM** for the colonial census rolls (especially 1724–1752) and any emigrant records if your ancestor arrived from France.
6. **Check published transcriptions** — scholars like Winston De Ville and Charles Maduell have transcribed many Superior Council and notarial records into English-language publications available in university libraries.

I'll build a comprehensive locality guide for Pennsylvania's coal mining regions in the late 1800s. Let me start by establishing the jurisdictional context — there were two distinct coal regions (anthracite in the northeast, bituminous in the southwest), so I'll survey the major counties in both.Good — I have standardPlace values for the key counties. Now I'll run the full parallel survey batch across the major anthracite (Schuylkill, Luzerne, Lackawanna) and bituminous (Westmoreland) coal counties plus the Pennsylvania state level.Now let me read the output format and breadth checklist references before compiling.I now have all the data needed to compile the guide. Here it is:

---

# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Coal Mining Towns (Late 1800s, c. 1850–1910)

## Jurisdiction Overview

Pennsylvania's coal country divides into two distinct regions, each with its own record universe:

**Anthracite (hard coal) region — Northeastern Pennsylvania**
The core counties are **Schuylkill** (formed 1811), **Luzerne**, **Lackawanna** (split from Luzerne in 1878), **Carbon**, and **Northumberland**. Major towns include Pottsville, Shenandoah, Mahanoy City, Shamokin (Schuylkill/Northumberland); Wilkes-Barre, Hazleton, Pittston (Luzerne); and Scranton, Carbondale (Lackawanna). The Philadelphia and Reading Coal and Iron Company and Lehigh Valley Coal Company were dominant employers.

**Bituminous (soft coal) region — Southwestern Pennsylvania**
**Westmoreland** (formed 1773), **Fayette**, **Washington**, and **Allegheny** counties. Connellsville coke ovens and Pittsburgh-area collieries employed large workforces connected to the iron and steel industry.

**Ethnic composition (late 1800s):** Early waves (1820s–1860s) were predominantly Irish and Welsh; Germans also arrived early. After 1880, massive immigration from Poland, Lithuania, Slovakia, Ukraine, and Italy transformed the coal patch towns. Each ethnic group typically worshipped in its own ethnic parish — a critical fact for genealogy.

**Key historical events:** Avondale Mine disaster (1869, 110 killed); Molly Maguires trials (Schuylkill County, 1876–1877); founding of the United Mine Workers of America (1890); anthracite strike (1902). Mine disasters were frequent throughout the period and generated official records with personal data.

---

## Boundary Changes ⚠️

**Lackawanna County did not exist before 1878** — it was carved from Luzerne County in that year. If your ancestor was in the Scranton or Carbondale area before 1878, their records (court, deed, probate, naturalization) will be found in **Luzerne County**, not Lackawanna. Treat these as one continuous research trail: search Luzerne County records for the pre-1878 period, then shift to Lackawanna after that date.

All other major coal counties were stable in their boundaries throughout the late 1800s.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Critical gap:** Pennsylvania did not require statewide registration of **births and deaths until 1906**. For most of the late 1800s, there is no civil birth or death record. The 1906–1968 Death Certificates collection on FamilySearch (4,218,763 indexed records with images) covers deaths just after your target period but is essential for miners who lived into the 1900s.
- **Marriage registration** began earlier at the county level. FamilySearch holds:
  - *Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950* — 524,189 indexed records with 312,045 images (**indexed + images**)
  - *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* — 312,045 indexed records with 156,023 images (**indexed + images**)
  - *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* — 198,765 indexed records, **no images** (index only)
- **For births and deaths 1850–1906: church records are your primary source** (see below).

### Church Records ⭐ Most Important Pre-1906 Source

Ethnic parishes are the backbone of coal mining genealogy. They recorded baptisms, marriages, and burials in detail — often including parents' village of origin in the Old Country. Key denominations by ethnicity:

| Ethnicity | Denomination | Language of Records |
|---|---|---|
| Irish | Roman Catholic | English/Latin |
| Welsh | Congregational / Presbyterian | English |
| German | Lutheran / Reformed / Catholic | German |
| Polish | Roman Catholic | Polish/Latin |
| Lithuanian | Roman Catholic | Lithuanian/Latin |
| Slovak | Roman Catholic / Lutheran | Slovak/Latin |
| Ukrainian | Greek Catholic / Orthodox | Ukrainian/Church Slavonic |
| Italian | Roman Catholic | Italian/Latin |

FamilySearch holds *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* — **1,245,830 records with 607,220 images** (**indexed + images**). However, coverage is uneven and many ethnic immigrant parishes from after 1880 are not yet digitized.

**Where to look beyond FamilySearch:** Contact the relevant diocesan archives directly (Diocese of Allentown covers Schuylkill/Carbon; Diocese of Scranton covers Luzerne/Lackawanna). Polish, Lithuanian, and Slovak parishes sometimes hold original registers; some have deposited them with the diocese or with FamilySearch. Records of Welsh Congregational/Presbyterian churches may be at the Presbyterian Historical Society in Philadelphia.

**Non-English records:** Slovak, Polish, Lithuanian, Ukrainian, and Italian registers require reading the original language and/or Latin. Factor this in when planning research.

### Census Records

| Year | Coverage | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1850, 1860 | Full population schedule | Lists head of household with ages/birthplaces for all family members |
| 1870 | Full population schedule | Occupation "miner" common; lists birthplace of parents |
| 1880 | Full population schedule | Most detailed 19th-c. census; lists relationship to head |
| 1890 | **Mostly destroyed** by fire | Special Union Veterans/Widows schedule survives; general population largely lost |
| 1900 | Full population schedule | Adds immigration year and years married |

A **special 1880 Census of Miners** (industrial schedules) was conducted for some coal counties — check NARA and the Pennsylvania State Archives.

Pennsylvania conducted **state censuses in 1850, 1855, 1860, 1865, 1870, 1875, and 1880**, but most are lost or incomplete. Do not rely on these as a gap-filler; the federal schedules are more complete.

Federal censuses 1850–1880 are **indexed + images** on FamilySearch (free) and Ancestry (subscription). The 1900 census is **indexed + images** on both. Ancestry's census collection is the most complete access point.

### Mining-Specific Occupational Records ⭐ Unique to This Region

These records exist nowhere else and can identify individuals, their nationalities, and their employment history. FamilySearch has digitized a limited number of volumes:

| Volume | County | Date Range | Images | Access Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mine Worker Employment Cards | Schuylkill | 1870–1900 | 410 | **Indexed + images** (95% name-indexed, full-text searchable) |
| Mine Accident Reports | Luzerne | 1870–1895 | 287 | **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text searchable — must be browsed image by image) |
| Colliery Payroll Ledgers | Lackawanna | 1878–1900 | 199 | **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text searchable) |

Beyond FamilySearch, the most important mining records are:

- **Pennsylvania Mine Inspector Annual Reports (1870 onward):** Published state documents listing every fatal accident by name, age, nationality, and cause of death. Held at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) and in printed volumes at the State Library of Pennsylvania. These are not fully digitized but are invaluable for identifying deceased miners.
- **Company employment records and payroll ledgers:** Survive fragmentarily. The Philadelphia and Reading Coal and Iron Company records are partly at the **Historical Society of Schuylkill County** (Pottsville). Additional company records are at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** and local historical societies. Extent of survival varies greatly by company.
- **United Mine Workers of America (UMWA) records (post-1890):** Membership rolls and grievance files. Contact the UMWA archives and the Pennsylvania State University Labor Archives (State College) which holds significant labor union collections.

### Probate and Court Records

FamilySearch holds *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* — **893,214 records with 446,607 images** (**indexed + images**). Coverage across coal counties varies; verify specific county coverage before relying on negative results.

Original probate files (wills, inventories, letters of administration) are held at **county courthouses** — Orphans' Court division. These often list all heirs by name and relationship, making them excellent for reconstructing families.

Court records (Quarter Sessions, Common Pleas) held at county courthouses contain naturalizations (critical for immigrants — see below), guardianships, and civil suits.

### Immigration and Naturalization Records ⭐ Essential for Immigrant Families

The vast majority of coal mining families in this period were immigrants or the children of immigrants. Naturalization and passenger records are frequently the best sources for Old Country origins.

**Ports of entry:**
- **Philadelphia** — Primary port for many early Welsh and Irish immigrants, and for later Eastern European arrivals
- **New York** (Castle Garden until 1892; Ellis Island from 1892) — Primary port for post-1880 immigration waves

**Naturalization (pre-1906):** Before the Bureau of Immigration centralized naturalization in 1906, any court of record could naturalize. Miners naturalized in whichever court was convenient — often the County Court of Common Pleas near the mines. **Schuylkill County naturalization records (1840s–1906) are at the Pennsylvania State Archives.** Look for both the Declaration of Intention ("first papers") and the Petition for Naturalization ("second papers") — they may be in different courts or different years.

**Post-1906 naturalizations:** Filed with federal courts; the records are at **NARA Philadelphia** (Northeast Region, Philadelphia Federal Records Center) or at NARA College Park. Many post-1906 petitions include detailed personal information, including place of birth in the Old Country.

**Passenger lists:** Indexed on Ancestry.com and FamilySearch. Pre-1895 lists are brief; post-1895 lists expand significantly; post-1906 lists (especially Ellis Island) are the most detailed. When searching, check all family members on the full manifest page — children are listed with parents.

NARA (www.archives.gov) holds the originals.

### Land and Tax Records

Coal mining families typically **did not own land** — they rented company housing in coal patch towns. Do not expect extensive deed records for miners themselves. However:

- **Tax records** (assessment lists) exist at county level and can serve as annual census substitutes. Lists are held at **county courthouses** and the **Pennsylvania State Archives**.
- If a family did acquire property, deeds are recorded at the **county Recorder of Deeds** office. Pennsylvania uses a metes-and-bounds land description system.
- **Company store ledger records** (the "truck system" of credit at the company store) survive in some company archives and show family purchases, debts, and implicitly household composition.

### Military Records

For the late 1800s, the primary conflict is the **Civil War (1861–1865)**, just preceding the main target period. Many miners who were in Pennsylvania by 1861 served or were drafted. Check:

- **Civil War service records and pension files:** NARA and **Fold3** (subscription). Pension files are especially rich, often containing affidavits naming family members, neighbors, and places of residence.
- **Civil War draft registration (1863):** Lists all men aged 20–45 — may capture immigrant miners by name even if they did not serve. On Ancestry and Fold3.
- **Pennsylvania Adjutant General records:** At the Pennsylvania State Archives.
- No major federal conflict occurred during the 1870s–1900s, but the **Spanish-American War (1898)** generated some service records at NARA.

### Cemetery Records

Coal mining communities established both denominational and non-denominational cemeteries. Ethnic Catholic cemeteries (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak sections) often have tombstone inscriptions in the heritage language and may record place of origin.

- **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) — Free; crowdsourced transcriptions and photos of headstones. Coverage is strong in Schuylkill and Luzerne counties but uneven in smaller coal patch communities.
- **BillionGraves** — Smaller alternative with GPS-tagged photos.
- **Sexton records** (burial registers) — Often held by the cemetery office or the maintaining church; many are not digitized. Contact individual cemeteries directly.

### Newspaper Records

Local papers published obituaries, accident notices, marriage announcements, and legal notices (naturalization intentions, sheriff's sales). Mining accidents often generated detailed reporting naming victims.

Key papers include the **Miners' Journal** (Pottsville, Schuylkill County — one of the oldest coal region papers), the **Wilkes-Barre Times-Leader**, and the **Scranton Tribune**.

- **Newspapers.com** (subscription) — Good coverage of Pennsylvania regional papers
- **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, free) — Selected Pennsylvania papers; check for your county
- **Pennsylvania Newspaper Archives** — Available through many Pennsylvania public libraries
- **Historical Society of Schuylkill County** and similar local societies hold bound runs of local papers

### DNA and Genetic Genealogy

For ancestors from tightly defined ethnic enclaves (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Irish), DNA testing at AncestryDNA or 23andMe can be productive. The concentrated immigrant communities in coal towns mean that distant cousins may appear as matches. Be aware that endogamy within ethnic communities (especially Lithuanian and Eastern European groups) can inflate shared DNA and complicate relationship estimation.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | What's available for this area | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Church and Town Records 1708–1985 (1.2M records); County Marriages 1885–1950 (524K); Civil Marriages 1677–1950 (312K); Probate Records 1683–1994 (893K); Death Certificates 1906–1968 (4.2M); Compiled Marriages 1700–1900 (198K); Mine Worker Employment Cards Schuylkill 1870–1900 (browse/search); Mine Accident Reports Luzerne 1870–1895 (browse-only); Colliery Payroll Ledgers Lackawanna 1878–1900 (browse-only) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** (ancestry.com) | U.S. Federal Census 1850–1940; passenger lists; naturalization indexes; PA Death Certificates; newspaper collections | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery and headstone records | Free |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Civil War service & pension records; draft registrations | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | PA regional papers including coal region | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov/newspapers) | Selected PA papers | Free |
| **NARA Online** (archives.gov) | Passenger lists; naturalization; military records | Free/fee varies |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Mine Inspector Annual Reports (1870+); naturalization records; state census; tax records; company records | In-person; some records via mail request; finding aids online |
| **Historical Society of Schuylkill County** (Pottsville) | Philadelphia & Reading Coal & Iron Company records; local newspapers; church records; photographs | In-person; contact for hours |
| **Anthracite Heritage Museum** (Scranton/McDade Park) | Mining community records; union records; immigrant community materials | In-person |
| **Penn State University Labor Archives** (State College) | UMWA records; labor union collections for coal region | In-person; finding aids online |
| **County Courthouses** (each county seat) | Deeds; probate; naturalizations; court records | In-person; some accessible by mail |
| **NARA Philadelphia Region** (Philadelphia) | Federal court naturalization records post-1906; some military records | In-person; online ordering for some records |
| **Diocesan Archives** (Diocese of Allentown; Diocese of Scranton) | Catholic parish registers, especially ethnic parishes | Write/contact for access policies |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia) | Welsh and other Presbyterian/Congregational church records | In-person; some records on FamilySearch |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following record classes exist but have limited or no online presence — do not assume they don't exist just because they don't appear in a database search:

- **Most company employment and payroll records** beyond the three FamilySearch volumes noted above
- **Mine Inspector Annual Reports** (only partially digitized)
- **County tax assessment lists** for most coal counties
- **Most ethnic parish registers** for post-1880 Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Ukrainian, and Italian parishes
- **Company store ledgers**
- **UMWA membership and grievance files**
- **Sexton/burial records** for smaller denominational cemeteries
- **County naturalization records** outside Schuylkill County (Luzerne, Lackawanna, Westmoreland originals are physical-only at their respective courthouses)

---

## Known Record Losses

- **1890 Federal Census:** Nearly all population schedules were destroyed by fire and water damage in 1921 and are not available for any Pennsylvania county. The surviving **Veterans and Widows special schedule** may capture Civil War veterans in your family.
- **Pre-statewide registration vital records (births and deaths before 1906):** These simply were not required to be created at the state level. Church registers are the primary substitute.
- **Some company records** were destroyed when mining companies closed, merged, or went bankrupt. The survival of corporate archives is uneven and often depends on whether a historical society acquired the papers.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Always check which county had jurisdiction** before beginning a search. If your ancestor was in Scranton/Carbondale before 1878, search Luzerne County records, not Lackawanna — they are a single continuous research trail split by the 1878 boundary change.

2. **Identify the ethnic parish first.** Because civil vital registration began only in 1906, church records will be your only source for births and deaths. Determine the ancestor's ethnicity and religion, then identify the specific ethnic parish serving their coal patch — that parish's register is more likely to record their family than the nearest generic Catholic church.

3. **Mine Inspector Reports are underused.** The annual reports (PA State Archives, from 1870) name every fatal mine accident victim with age and nationality. If your ancestor died in a mine, this may be the only record of his death for the pre-1906 period.

4. **Naming patterns matter for immigrants.** Polish, Slovak, and Lithuanian names were often anglicized or misspelled on arrival. A "Joseph Kowalczyk" may appear as "Joseph Cowalchek" in an American record. Search broadly and phonetically, especially in census and naturalization records.

5. **Naturalization was local before 1906.** Check the County Court of Common Pleas closest to where your ancestor lived — not just federal records. Schuylkill County records are at the PA State Archives; other counties' originals are at the respective courthouses.

6. **Use the 1880 census carefully.** It is the richest pre-1900 census (lists relationship to head of household) and the last complete census before the 1890 destruction. An ancestor "not found" between 1880 and 1900 may have arrived after 1880; check passenger lists and the 1900 census's "year of immigration" column.

7. **Consult the FamilySearch Wiki article** "Pennsylvania Occupational Records — Coal Mining" (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Occupational_Records#Coal_Mining) for current guidance on record availability and access.

8. **For bituminous region (Westmoreland/Fayette/Washington counties):** The record universe is similar but company archives differ. The **Pennsylvania State Archives** and **Westmoreland County Historical Society** (Greensburg) are your primary physical repositories. The Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh holds strong collections for the western coal/steel region.

9. **Research sequence suggestion:** Start with the 1880 and 1900 federal censuses to anchor the family in a specific township and coal patch → identify the ethnic parish → search church registers → check the Mine Inspector Reports if a death is suspected → search naturalization records and passenger lists for Old Country origins → consult company records at historical societies if a specific employer is known.

# 📜 Locality Guide: Massachusetts, 1620–1700
### Early Puritan Colonial Period

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Two separate colonial jurisdictions operated within modern Massachusetts during this window — and their records must be treated as distinct trails:

| Jurisdiction | Dates | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Plymouth Colony** | 1620–1691 | Separatist (Pilgrim) settlement; merged into Province of Massachusetts Bay in 1691 |
| **Massachusetts Bay Colony** | 1628–1691 | Puritan settlement; granted royal charter 1629 |
| **Province of Massachusetts Bay** | 1691–1776 | Combined charter; unified record-keeping |

Massachusetts was divided into its first four counties in **1643** — **Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, and Norfolk** — meaning county-level records (deeds, probate, courts) begin at that date. Before 1643, colonial-level courts and the General Court held jurisdiction.

The Wikipedia article on the *Massachusetts Bay Colony* notes the colony required towns to record vital events starting in **1639**, making it one of the earliest mandated civil-registration systems in North America.

---

## Population & Research Scale

No historical population data was returned for this period by FamilySearch's population tool. From external sources, the Great Migration brought approximately **20,000 settlers** to Massachusetts Bay between 1630 and 1640, with Plymouth Colony adding several thousand more. This relatively small founding population means prosopographical (every-name) research tools like the *Great Migration* series are practical and heavily used.

---

## Record-by-Record Survey

### 1. 🏛️ Town Vital Records (Births, Marriages, Deaths)
**Coverage:** 1620–1890 | **FamilySearch Collection:** [Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2061550) (ID 2061550)

- The colony mandated recording of births, marriages, and deaths at the **town level** from **1639** onward — the earliest continuous civil registration in what became the United States.
- FamilySearch holds this collection with **~3.4 million records** and images.
- The published **"Vital Records of [Town Name] to 1850"** series (the Tanner–Corbin series) abstracts town clerk records for **200+ Massachusetts towns** and is widely available in print at genealogical libraries. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Massachusetts Genealogy — Colonial Vital Records")*
- **Access:** Indexed on FamilySearch (searchable by name). Original town clerk volumes also survive at individual **Town Clerk offices** and in many cases at the **Massachusetts Archives**.
- **Gap:** Plymouth Colony towns (Plymouth, Duxbury, Scituate, etc.) are recorded in the Plymouth Colony records (see Court Records below) rather than in Bay Colony town records for the pre-1691 period.

---

### 2. ⛪ Congregational Church Records
**Coverage:** 1630–1920 | **FamilySearch Collection:** [Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921657) (ID 1921657)

- Congregationalism was the **established church** of Massachusetts Bay Colony. Church records are therefore *civic as well as religious* for this period.
- These registers contain: **membership admissions, baptisms, marriages, disciplinary proceedings** (excommunications, admonishments), and sometimes burials.
- FamilySearch holds **~987,000 records** with images across this collection.
- Many early church records have been published in the **Colonial Society of Massachusetts** series and by local historical societies. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Massachusetts Church Records — Congregational")*
- **Access:** Indexed on FamilySearch. Published transcriptions often available through NEHGS and academic libraries.
- **Important note:** Plymouth Colony settlers were Separatists, not Congregationalists — their church records are distinct and held within Plymouth Colony court/church records.

---

### 3. ⚖️ Probate Records
**Coverage:** 1635–1991 | **FamilySearch Collection:** [Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678901) (ID 1678901)

- Probate began at the colonial/General Court level from the **1630s**, then shifted to county probate courts after county formation in 1643.
- FamilySearch holds **~567,000 records** with images.
- Probate files often contain **estate inventories** — extremely valuable for the 17th century as they list household goods, debts, land, and sometimes names of heirs and neighbors.
- **Access:** Indexed on FamilySearch. Original volumes held at county **Probate and Family Courts** and the **Massachusetts Archives**.

---

### 4. 📄 Land Records (Deeds)
**FamilySearch Coverage:** Not surfaced as a standalone indexed collection in this survey.

- Land recording at the **county level** began with county formation in **1643**. Pre-1643 land grants were recorded in the Massachusetts Bay Colony General Court records.
- County **Registries of Deeds** hold deed books from the 1630s–1640s in "remarkably complete runs." *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Massachusetts Genealogy")*
- Plymouth Colony land records are published separately.
- **Access:** County registries are the primary physical repository. Many early deed books have been microfilmed by FamilySearch; check the FamilySearch catalog by county. The **Massachusetts Archives** holds some colonial-era land grants. Digitized deed indexes are available at individual county registry websites (e.g., Suffolk Deeds online).
- **Digitization level:** Likely browse-only microfilm for much of this period — no standalone indexed FamilySearch collection surfaced in this survey. Verify in the FamilySearch catalog.

---

### 5. 🏛️ Colonial Court Records & Plymouth Colony Records
**FamilySearch Coverage:** Partially within the Town Vital Records and Church Records collections; Plymouth Colony records are published separately.

- The **Massachusetts Bay Colony General Court** records from the 1620s onward are held at the **Massachusetts Archives** in Boston, including governor's correspondence, court cases, and legislative acts.
- **Plymouth Colony records (1620–1691)** are published and indexed separately as the *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* (12 volumes, ed. Nathaniel Shurtleff and David Pulsifer). These cover court proceedings, land transactions, vital events, and church matters. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Massachusetts Genealogy")*
- Court records are valuable for names of witnesses, sureties, creditors, and neighbors — expanding the FAN (Friends, Associates, Neighbors) cluster around any individual.

---

### 6. 🚢 Immigration & Passenger Records
**FamilySearch Passenger Lists Collection:** Covers 1820–1891 — entirely **outside** the 1620–1700 target window; do not use for this period.

For 17th-century arrivals, the primary tools are:
- **The Great Migration series** (NEHGS): Robert Charles Anderson's multi-volume prosopography documenting every identified settler arriving 1620–1643. This is the premier reference for this period, with source citations for each individual.
- **The Winthrop Fleet passenger lists** (1630): several ships' names and passenger rosters have been reconstructed from English and colonial sources.
- English parish records (baptisms, marriages) for settlers' home counties — primarily East Anglia (Suffolk, Essex, Norfolk), Lincolnshire, and Kent — are the starting point to trace origins back to England.

---

### 7. 🪖 Military Records (Colonial Militia & King Philip's War)
**FamilySearch Coverage:** Fold3 is listed as a curated external link but covers primarily later periods. No specific 1620–1700 military collection surfaced in the FamilySearch indexed catalog.

- Colonial militia muster rolls and records of **King Philip's War (1675–1676)** are a significant source for male heads of household.
- Published sources: *Massachusetts Soldiers and Sailors of the Revolutionary War* does not cover this period, but the **Massachusetts Archives** holds colonial military lists.
- The **New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS)** holds manuscript collections related to King Philip's War.

---

### 8. ⛪ Substitute Sources & Finding Aids

Because this is a pre-census era (the first federal census is 1790), and some town records are sparse or destroyed, researchers should layer these substitute sources:

| Source | What It Captures |
|---|---|
| **Tax / Rate Lists** | Names of property-owning heads of household |
| **Freeman Admission Lists** | Adult males admitted as freemen of the colony (essentially a voter roll) |
| **Church Membership Lists** | Admission dates, sometimes with ages and origins |
| **Town Meeting Minutes** | Land grants, appointments, disputes |
| **Gravestone Inscriptions** | Births/deaths, especially in older burying grounds; FindAGrave is a curated resource |
| **Published Genealogies** | NEHGS *Register* (published since 1847) contains thousands of New England family histories |

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Massachusetts Archives** (Boston) | Colonial court records, General Court records, governor's correspondence from 1620s; original town vital records | In-person; some digitized |
| **New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS)** (Boston) | Largest genealogical library in New England; Great Migration series; manuscript collections; NEHGS Register | In-person + online subscription (AmericanAncestors.org) |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Town Vital Records 1620–1890; Church Records 1630–1920; Probate Records 1635–1991 | Free online |
| **County Registries of Deeds** (Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, etc.) | Deed books from 1640s onward | In-person; some digitized at county websites |
| **County Probate Courts** | Original probate files from 1640s | In-person; FamilySearch microfilm |
| **National Archives (NARA)** | Federal records (mostly post-1700 for this region) | In-person + online |
| **Plymouth County records / Pilgrim Society** | Plymouth Colony-specific records | In-person (Plymouth, MA); published volumes |

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Type | FamilySearch Availability | Access Level |
|---|---|---|
| Town Vital Records (1620–1890) | ✅ Collection 2061550 | Indexed + images |
| Church Records (1630–1920) | ✅ Collection 1921657 | Indexed + images |
| Probate Records (1635–1991) | ✅ Collection 1678901 | Indexed + images |
| Land / Deed Records | ⚠️ No standalone indexed collection found | Likely microfilm/browse-only; verify in FamilySearch catalog by county |
| Colonial Court Records | ⚠️ Partial; Plymouth Colony records published in print | Physical + published volumes |
| Passenger / Immigration Records | ❌ FamilySearch lists begin 1820 | Use Great Migration series (NEHGS) |
| Military (Militia / King Philip's War) | ⚠️ No indexed collection found for this period | Massachusetts Archives; NEHGS manuscripts |

---

## Key Finding Aid: The *Great Migration* Series

The single most important reference tool for 1620–1643 settlers is Robert Charles Anderson's ***The Great Migration: Immigrants to New England, 1634–1635*** and related volumes (NEHGS). It documents every known settler who arrived in Massachusetts in this period with sources, family reconstructions, and English origins. It is available in print through NEHGS and partially through their online database at **AmericanAncestors.org**. If you're tracing a family to the Puritan settlements, begin here before searching record by record.

---

**Ready for the next step?** If you have a specific surname or town in mind, I can build a targeted research plan — or execute searches in the FamilySearch collections identified above.

# Oklahoma Territory — Locality Guide for the Land Run Era (c. 1880–1910)

## Jurisdictional Context

This period spans **two overlapping jurisdictions** that researchers must keep clearly separated:

| Jurisdiction | Dates | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Indian Territory** | Pre-statehood | Governed by the Five Civilized Tribes; no standard county structure for non-Indians |
| **Oklahoma Territory** | May 2, 1890 – Nov. 16, 1907 | Created by the Organic Act; capital at Guthrie; standard county courts and land offices |
| **State of Oklahoma** | Nov. 16, 1907 – present | Merger of both territories; FamilySearch standardPlace begins here |

The Land Run of 1889 (April 22) opened the **Unassigned Lands** before Oklahoma Territory even existed as a formal jurisdiction — meaning the earliest settlers filed homestead claims through the **General Land Office**, not a territorial government. Land offices in **Guthrie** and **Kingfisher** processed 1889 applications. Four subsequent runs (1891, 1892, 1893, 1895) opened additional sections. Knowing which run (if any) your ancestor participated in, and whether they lived in Oklahoma Territory or Indian Territory, determines which record sets apply.

> ⚠️ **Key research decision:** If your ancestor lived east of the line separating the two territories (i.e., in the Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, or Seminole Nations), their records are **tribal and federal court records**, not county courthouse records. The Indian Territory and Oklahoma Territory record streams only merged at statehood in 1907.

---

## Record Classes, Availability, and Access

### 1. Land & Homestead Records ⭐ *Prime source for this period*

**Why essential:** For Land Run settlers, the homestead file is often the single richest genealogical document — containing the original application, proof of five-years' residence (signed by witnesses who knew the family), ages, and occasionally birthplaces.

| Source | What's There | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **BLM General Land Office** — [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov) | Final patents, searchable by name; free | Online, free |
| **NARA, Record Group 49** | Full homestead application case files — application, proof, witness testimony, rejected applications | Physical at NARA Washington, D.C.; some digitized via FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| **FamilySearch — "Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907"** | 234,567 records / 117,283 images — covers territorial land transactions | [Collection 1541872](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872) — indexed with images |

> Note: Rejected homestead applications survive alongside approved ones and can be equally valuable — they often contain family details even when the claim failed.

---

### 2. Census Records

**Situation:** The 1890 federal census was largely destroyed by fire — but **Oklahoma Territory has a rare surviving exception**.

| Record | Coverage | Location |
|---|---|---|
| **1890 Oklahoma Territory Special Census** | One of the very few surviving 1890 census records in the nation; documents the first wave of Land Run settlers | **Oklahoma Historical Society** (okhistory.org); also partially on FamilySearch |
| **1900 Federal Census** | Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory enumerated separately | Ancestry.com; FamilySearch |
| **1910 Federal Census** | State of Oklahoma (post-statehood) | Ancestry.com; FamilySearch |
| **Territorial/state mortality schedules** | Deaths in intercensal years | Oklahoma Historical Society; NARA |
| **County tax rolls, 1890s** | Document early settlers year-by-year between censuses | Oklahoma Historical Society; county courthouses |

> ⚠️ Do not assume the 1890 Oklahoma Territory Special Census is complete — coverage varies by county district. Tax rolls from the 1890s are a critical substitute for years without census data.

---

### 3. Indian Censuses, Tribal Rolls & the Dawes Roll

**Essential for anyone with Native American ancestry or who intermarried with tribal members.**

| Record | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch — "Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959"** | 567,890 records / 283,945 images — annual tribal censuses and enrollment rolls | [Collection 1987432](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432) — indexed with images |
| **Dawes Rolls (1898–1914)** | Enrollment of Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, and Seminole Nation members for allotment purposes — required to receive land allotments | NARA (RG 75); Ancestry; FamilySearch; Oklahoma Historical Society |
| **Freedmen Rolls** | Formerly enslaved members of the Five Nations | Same repositories as Dawes Rolls |

> Note: Many Land Run settlers married into tribal families or interacted with Indian Territory residents — even if your ancestor was not Native American, tribal records may name them as witnesses, neighbors, or in-laws.

---

### 4. Marriage Records

| Record | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch — "Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995"** | 456,789 records / 913,578 persons / 228,394 images — county-level registration from territorial period onward | [Collection 2134789](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134789) — indexed with images |
| **Indian Territory marriage records** | Handled by tribal courts and the U.S. Court for the Indian Territory (Muskogee) before 1907 | NARA at Fort Worth; Oklahoma State Archives |

> ⚠️ Oklahoma did **not** require statewide vital registration until **1908** (after statehood). For marriages 1889–1907, county records are your best source — but for Indian Territory residents, look to tribal and federal court records instead.

---

### 5. Church & Mission Records

| Record | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch — "Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965"** | 123,456 records / 61,728 images — Methodist, Baptist, and Presbyterian mission registers, many predating the Land Run in Indian Territory | [Collection 1654321](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321) — indexed with images |
| **Denomination archives** | Baptist, Methodist, and Presbyterian churches established congregations in new Oklahoma Territory towns from 1889 onward | Contact individual denominational archives (e.g., American Baptist Historical Society) |

> Church records are especially important for **1889–1907 vital events** (births, deaths) because civil registration was not yet required. Oklahoma Territory church records begin sporadically from 1889 as congregations formed in the new towns.

---

### 6. Territorial Court Records

Oklahoma Territory's district courts handled probate, naturalization, and civil/criminal matters 1890–1907. Indian Territory had a separate **U.S. Court for the Indian Territory** at Muskogee.

| Record | What's There | Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Oklahoma Territory District Court files** | Probate, naturalizations (pre-1906 scattered across any court of record), civil/criminal cases naming family members | **National Archives at Fort Worth** (NARA Southwest Region); **Oklahoma State Archives** |
| **U.S. Court for the Indian Territory** | Equity, civil, probate, and criminal for Indian Territory residents | **National Archives at Fort Worth** |

> ⚠️ Pre-1906 naturalizations can appear in **any court of record** — federal, territorial, or local. Search all courts for the county/district where your ancestor lived.

---

### 7. Vital Records (Births & Deaths)

**Major gap:** Oklahoma did not implement **statewide birth and death registration until 1908**. For the 1889–1907 period:
- Look to **church records** (baptisms, burials)
- **County death registers** (some counties kept their own, inconsistently)
- **Newspaper notices** (obituaries, birth announcements — see below)
- **Probate files** (often name decedents, dates, and survivors)

After 1908: Oklahoma State Department of Health holds statewide registrations (access through the **Oklahoma State Archives**).

---

### 8. Military Records

| Record | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **NARA service records & pension files** | Civil War veterans who settled in Oklahoma (many Land Run participants were veterans); Spanish-American War (1898) Oklahoma volunteers | NARA (archives.gov); Fold3 |
| **Fold3** | Digitized military service records, draft registrations | [fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com) (subscription) |
| **Oklahoma Adjutant General records** | State militia and National Guard from 1907 | Oklahoma Military Department / Oklahoma State Archives |

---

### 9. Newspapers

Oklahoma had active territorial newspapers from the day of the Land Run — the *Guthrie Daily Leader* and the first Oklahoma City papers were founded in April 1889.

| Source | Access |
|---|---|
| **Newspapers.com** | Large collection of Oklahoma territorial and early-state papers | [newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com) (subscription) |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society Digital Collections** | Territorial newspapers digitized, including some free access | [okhistory.org](https://www.okhistory.org) |
| **Chronicling America (Library of Congress)** | Some free digitized Oklahoma territorial papers | [chroniclingamerica.loc.gov](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) |

---

### 10. Cemetery Records

| Source | Access |
|---|---|
| **Find A Grave** | County and church cemeteries established from 1889 onward | [findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com) (free) |
| **BillionGraves** | Additional cemetery transcriptions | billiongraves.com |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** | County cemetery surveys | okhistory.org |

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | Strengths for This Period |
|---|---|
| **BLM General Land Office** — glorecords.blm.gov | Homestead patents; free; searchable by name |
| **NARA Washington, D.C.** (RG 49) | Full homestead application files |
| **National Archives at Fort Worth** (NARA Southwest) | Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory court records |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (okhistory.org) | 1890 special census; territorial newspapers; tax rolls; state records |
| **Oklahoma State Archives** (OKgov) | Vital records post-1908; territorial and state court records |
| **FamilySearch** | 4 indexed Oklahoma collections covering land, marriages, tribal rolls, church records |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census; some homestead records |
| **Fold3** | Military records |

---

## Critical Research Strategy: Trace Origins *Before* Oklahoma

Per the FamilySearch wiki article **"Oklahoma Land Records"** and **"Oklahoma Territorial Records"**: most Land Run settlers came from **Kansas, Texas, Missouri, and Arkansas**. For many families, the Oklahoma trail starts in 1889 but the genealogical story begins a generation earlier in one of those states. Plan to search departure-state records (county deed indexes, church registers, census records) alongside Oklahoma records to identify who the family was before they joined the run.

---

## Coverage Gaps to Note

- **Volume-level digitization percentages** could not be assessed — the volume search tool was unavailable in this session. Some FamilySearch collections listed above may include browse-only images for specific counties; verify before assuming full name-indexed coverage.
- **Wiki place pages** for Oklahoma returned only generic content — consult the FamilySearch Wiki article [Oklahoma Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Oklahoma_Genealogy) directly for the most current guidance.
- **1889–1890 gap**: Oklahoma Territory wasn't formally organized until May 1890 — the 13 months between the Land Run and territorial organization left a thin documentary record. The GLO homestead files and the 1890 special census are your best anchors for this window.
- **Indian Territory vs. Oklahoma Territory**: These were legally separate until 1907. Confirm which side of the line your ancestor lived on before choosing which court and land record set to search.
